```
<snip>
For example: You PERFORM CA until X1 is > 256 then drop through CA. As

poor programming.

You also abuse the language. You exit the performed paragraph CB with
a GO TO CC. This could result in the perform stack being exceeded
resulting in program failure. It is unlikely that you have reached
that with this program, but it is indiicative of your lack of any
defensive coding style.

[pl]

That isn't necessarily the case.  Using a stack isn't the only way of doing
this.

As for the rest: his style makes me itch but as the code does what is
required of it - it works, he understands it, and he finds it easy to
maintain (and he's the only one that has to be considered) - nothing else
needs to be considered .  So he uses uper-case letters.  So?  I do too.
Just that I'm used to doing it that way.

{/pl]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-09-01T12:06:50-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e815f30d-40b6-41f9-bffe-1d4a1f31f721@o40g2000prn.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <5141cde0-ff72-41de-a456-ec8e1e3da5b3@v39g2000pro.googlegroups.com> <NMVuk.10406$3A4.8304@newsfe04.iad>`

```
On Sep 2, 5:38 am, "tlmfru" <la...@mts.net> wrote:
> Richard <rip...@azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> this.

Which is why I said: "could".

However, he claimed to be using Microfocus, I understand that this
does use a perform stack. In fact there is even an error code 168 for
this. Whether this particular code would cause the stack to overflow
with a code 168 I don't know, but it is not the sort of code that I
would allow.


> As for the rest: his style makes me itch but as the code does what is
> required of it - it works, he understands it, and he finds it easy to
> maintain (and he's the only one that has to be considered) - nothing else
> needs to be considered .

The program does not do what is claimed. Foodman claims that it
extracts "the names of all images". It does not. It only extracts a
maximum of one per line, and only those where the src attribute
follows the tag identifer by exactly one space.

He also claims that it writes a bat file he can "use to copy the
files". It will not. Web servers use an offset file system to contain
access to be within a subset of the disk file system. So, for example
'<img src="/images/pic.jpeg">' will actually refer to a file such as: /
var/www/html/images/pic.jpeg. The bat file output from the program
will contain "COPY /IMAGES/PIC.JPEG" which is useless for several
reasons:

1) COPY is a DOS thing
2) for DOS/Windows the '/' should be '\'
3) the server offset prefix is missing
4) there is no destination (which may be OK on DOS)


> So he uses uper-case letters.  So?  I do too.
> Just that I'm used to doing it that way.

I haven't commented at all yet on the 'style' (except it lacks
defensive code).

> {/pl]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT? Implementation of in-line PERFORMS (was Programming Contest)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net>
- **Date:** 2008-09-02T13:11:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ilfvk.12815$3A4.7649@newsfe04.iad>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <5141cde0-ff72-41de-a456-ec8e1e3da5b3@v39g2000pro.googlegroups.com> <NMVuk.10406$3A4.8304@newsfe04.iad> <e815f30d-40b6-41f9-bffe-1d4a1f31f721@o40g2000prn.googlegroups.com>`

```
This isn't strictly a Cobol question which is why it's OT(?).

In Intel-style assembler, calls are implemented through the stack.  In
IBM-style assembler, they are implemented by a BAL (branch-and-link)
instruction, and the first step in the called routine is to save the return
address which the BAL provides.

My questions:

- there must be a similar concept in Intel - access to the "current address"
that the program is executing at
- if so, it could be used to implement calls without using the stack
- if so, is there any reason why it shouldn't be used?

(Not trying to promote a revolution here.  Just curious).

PL




Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:e815f30d-40b6-41f9-bffe->
> That isn't necessarily the case. Using a stack isn't the only way of doing
> this.

Which is why I said: "could".

However, he claimed to be using Microfocus, I understand that this
does use a perform stack. In fact there is even an error code 168 for
this. Whether this particular code would cause the stack to overflow
with a code 168 I don't know, but it is not the sort of code that I
would allow.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT? Implementation of in-line PERFORMS (was Programming Contest)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-09-02T22:09:42-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pgtrb418tts06klsc6cj9usirretrvusb6@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <5141cde0-ff72-41de-a456-ec8e1e3da5b3@v39g2000pro.googlegroups.com> <NMVuk.10406$3A4.8304@newsfe04.iad> <e815f30d-40b6-41f9-bffe-1d4a1f31f721@o40g2000prn.googlegroups.com> <ilfvk.12815$3A4.7649@newsfe04.iad>`

```
On Tue, 2 Sep 2008 13:11:00 -0500, "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:

>This isn't strictly a Cobol question which is why it's OT(?).
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>that the program is executing at

No, CALL is the only way to get the IP (instruction pointer), which is Intel's PSW.

>- if so, it could be used to implement calls without using the stack

You could do it this way:
call $+3
pop ax
add ax, 7?
jmp target


target:
.
.
jmp ax

- or - 

call target


target:
  pop   ax
 .
 .
 jmp ax

>- if so, is there any reason why it shouldn't be used?

Mainframe programs usually save the return address in memory. Intel's CALL does that for
you.  ENTER and LEAVE reduce stack housekeeping for parameters and temporary variables to
single instructions. 

Did you know IBM mainframes now have native stacks!? I mean hardware instructions to PUSH
and POP.  Before they got the instructions, they built their own stacks by chain linking
144-byte save areas.  Of course, Real Mainframe Programmers will continue doing it the Old
Way until someone prys the green card from their cold dead fingers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT? Implementation of in-line PERFORMS (was Programming Contest)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2008-09-03T12:06:18-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g9mgsn01l99@news1.newsguy.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<ilfvk.12815$3A4.7649@newsfe04.iad>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <5141cde0-ff72-41de-a456-ec8e1e3da5b3@v39g2000pro.googlegroups.com> <NMVuk.10406$3A4.8304@newsfe04.iad> <e815f30d-40b6-41f9-bffe-1d4a1f31f721@o40g2000prn.googlegroups.com> <ilfvk.12815$3A4.7649@newsfe04.iad>`

```
tlmfru wrote:
> 
> In Intel-style assembler, calls are implemented through the stack.  In
> IBM-style assembler, they are implemented by a BAL (branch-and-link)
> instruction, and the first step in the called routine is to save the return
> address which the BAL provides.

Yes. Most modern processor architectures follow one of these two
models: a contiguous call stack, or (possibly non-contiguous)
activation records. The majority use stacks.

> - there must be a similar concept in Intel - access to the "current address"
> that the program is executing at

Sure. The EIP (Extended Instruction Pointer) register is not directly
available to the operations that read the general-purpose registers,
but you can read the previous value (actually, the return address, so
the previous value plus the size of a call instruction) off the stack.
So by calling a minimal subroutine that copies its return address from
the stack to a general-purpose register, you can get the EIP value of
the instruction after the call.

(There are other, more arcane ways to do it; for example, use the
single-step flag and have the debug handler copy the execution address
into a register. But the call method is the simplest I know of offhand.)

> - if so, it could be used to implement calls without using the stack

Yes.

> - if so, is there any reason why it shouldn't be used?

Several:

- The Intel x86 ABI says that you use the stack. Do anything else, and
you're incompatible with every other piece of native code written for
that processor.

- Getting the EIP value on x86 is expensive, because you have to use a
call, as explained above.

- You have to allocate your activation frames from somewhere. If you
allocate them from the stack, you're using the stack, so you haven't
achieved anything different from the normal stack-based call
mechanism. If you allocate them from somewhere else (eg a heap), you
need to implement some kind of memory management. That's a bunch of
work, and expensive at runtime.

- x86 is optimized for stack-based calling. The push and pop
instructions are fast. The "leave" instruction is fast, and the
"enter" and "ret" instructions are relatively fast.

- Primitive contiguous stacks are inherently fast, which is why
they're popular for microprocessors, which is where the x86 family
comes from. Allocation and deallocation are just a matter of updating
a single register. Access to on-stack data is just a matter of using a
base-register-plus-offset memory-access mode, or if that's not
available, a register store followed by an immediate-mode add and a
memory load.

There are various advantages to activation records, because they're
more flexible than stacks, but they're more expensive, particularly
when stacks are easy to optimize in the processor.
```

```
In article <92fcce4e-2525-4bc8-a4ef-aeff6ac7a50a@n33g2000pri.googlegroups.com>,
Richard  <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>My answer here is yes, If I were to change the code I might
>want to start with a conditional, such as (in a theoretical general
>case): "IF X1 < 100". Then I would indent the remaining code, BUT, I
>would also have to examine all the code seeking out full stops.

What's this... if you desire to change the depth of condition the current 
design of the code would force you to go through the rest of it, line by 
line, making sure your change did not introduce any unanticipated errors?

Horrors!  *Everyone* knows that well-designed code allows for the depth of 
condition to be altered at a moment's notice, with no consideration 
whatsoever for what's going on around it... all ya gotta do is slap in the 
change and be done with it.

>
>You may think of this explanation as 'idiotic'. I think that
>scattering pointless full stops, and especially the useless commas,
>through the code as idiotic.

Styles differ, Mr Plinston; I was taugh, e'er-so-long ago, that the 
intention of COBOL was to be English-like to the point where a period/full 
stop was to signal the end of a sentence, the completion of a thought, the 
full conveying of a particular set of instructions.

Code - with some exceptions - is written for a variety of audiences.  One 
of those audiences is composed of those-who-maintain.  My experience is 
limited, I admit, but I have never heard those-who-maintain grouse or 
bellyache that 'the sentences are too clear and the code is too simple'.

DD
```

- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <92fcce4e-2525-4bc8-a4ef-aeff6ac7a50a@n33g2000pri.googlegroups.com> <g9fd5c$min$1@reader1.panix.com> <88af676a-5ab8-44cb-a9ac-b06fb0f76e12@w1g2000prk.googlegroups.com> <g9gvu1$31h$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Sep 2, 2:58 am, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
> In article <88af676a-5ab8-44cb-a9ac-b06fb0f76...@w1g2000prk.googlegroups.com>,
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Changing indentation is a trivial procedure consisting of merely
marking the range and a keystroke. Ensuring that the indentation
matches the logic levels is more important and also more arduous,
unless this has already been catered for in the original code.

>
> >And, for example, if the IF were later removed and the code
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> control - to what extent is another matter - what you do yourself.

There was no mention of 'control', merely an observation of what some
may feel necessary.


> >"""If you don't know what you are doing then do it neatly."""
>
> Neatness is also a matter of style, Mr Plinston.  Would you say a
> ninety-degree angle is more neat than a sixty-degree one?

It my code lines were displayed at such angles to the vertical then I
may form an opinion on that.


> >> Horrors!  *Everyone* knows that well-designed code allows for the depth of
> >> condition to be altered at a moment's notice, with no consideration
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> timesheets smile.

Your ideals may well be completely different from mine. For the most
part I am not paid by the number of hours that I spend working on the
code, thus I find ways to spend less time producing the required level
of usability and reliability. As I am also 'the next programmer' on
all the code, I have, over the years, determined that it is worth
while spending additional time on designing the structure and writing
the initial code such that it can be reused and maintained with
minimum effort.

Hence I try to defend against future changes.


> >> >You may think of this explanation as 'idiotic'. I think that
> >> >scattering pointless full stops, and especially the useless commas,
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> describe a matter of style as 'idiotic';

I was replying to a statement that called a particular style
'idiotic', do try and stay in context.

> idiot, according tohttp://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/idiot, is a term which is
> 'usually offensive'.
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> only') you practise and advocate a subset of possibilities.

I also practice subsets of other possibilities. I see no need, for
example, to use every Cobol verb or sub-clause in each program, or
indeed, for some, to use them at all.


> Your reasons for doing this are your own, of course... but to call
> something other than the particular subset you've chosen 'idiotic' does
> not, to me, appear to be a reasoned or dispassioned evaluation.

You completely missing the point seems to be par for the course.


> [snip]
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> brave of you to admit it!

Well of course they would complain, they would make far less money on
a per-hour basis, or even none at all.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-09-02T01:19:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g9i4a8$e14$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <88af676a-5ab8-44cb-a9ac-b06fb0f76e12@w1g2000prk.googlegroups.com> <g9gvu1$31h$1@reader1.panix.com> <6c4db269-d581-42b0-a52a-750f416462d8@t1g2000pra.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <6c4db269-d581-42b0-a52a-750f416462d8@t1g2000pra.googlegroups.com>,
Richard  <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>On Sep 2, 2:58�am, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
>> In article <88af676a-5ab8-44cb-a9ac-b06fb0f76...@w1g2000prk.googlegroups.com>,
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>marking the range and a keystroke.

Odd that you'd seem to feel a need to comment on what you call 'a trivial 
procedure', Mr Plinston... what do you hope to gain by pointing out 
trivialities?

>Ensuring that the indentation
>matches the logic levels is more important and also more arduous,
>unless this has already been catered for in the original code.

Ensuring that indentation of any kind is of questionable necessity in 
free-form code, Mr Plinston, and what 'has already been coatere for in the 
original code' seems perilously close to Hopper's denigrated 'But we've 
*always* done it this way!'

>
>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>may feel necessary.

When 'some might feel necessary' to comment, Mr Plinston, they might 
choose to do so.  What might be of interest - or at least a Good, Hearty 
Laugh - is to post what *you* feel necessary and let the un-named 'some' 
speak for themselves.

>
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>may form an opinion on that.

Neatness exists outside of code, Mr Plinston, as you pointed out in your 
assertion about it... which contained nary a mention of code.  Second 
request, then: would you say a ninety-degree angle is more neat than a 
sixty-degree one?

[snip]

>> >Of course no code is ideal, some less so than others.
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Your ideals may well be completely different from mine.

No matter what are ideals might be, Mr Plinston, if your assertion that 
'no code is ideal' then neither yours nor mine is.

[anip]

>> >> Styles differ, Mr Plinston;
>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>'idiotic', do try and stay in context.

Another person making a statement jumps off the Brooklyn Bridge, Mr 
Plinston... so you must, as well?  Please try to stay grounded.

[snip]

>> >> I was taugh, e'er-so-long ago, that the
>> >> intention of COBOL was to be English-like to the point where a period/full
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>indeed, for some, to use them at all.

It was never suggested that such would be a good thing, Mr Plinston... but 
please, don't let that fact deter you from making the argument.  Is it 
your honest opinion that there's an all-or-none approach to style and that 
differing views on the use of periods/full stops are to be equated with 
differing views on ALTER or RENAMES?

>> Your reasons for doing this are your own, of course... but to call
>> something other than the particular subset you've chosen 'idiotic' does
>> not, to me, appear to be a reasoned or dispassioned evaluation.
>
>You completely missing the point seems to be par for the course.

The point I see you trying to make, Mr Plinston, is that you've found a 
Very Good Reason to develop a certain style.  My question, quite simply, 
is: do you believe that there exist other Very Good Reasons, equally as 
valid as yours, which might result in code that shows a style other than 
yours?

>> >That _may_ be because they haven't seen my code.
>>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>a per-hour basis, or even none at all.

I'm not sure how you are using 'they would', Mr Plinston... if it 
indicates a subjunctive ('were they to maintain it they would') then you 
appear to be generating a situation which does not exist.

DD
```

```
On Tue, 2 Sep 2008 01:19:04 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>The point I see you trying to make, Mr Plinston, is that you've found a 
>Very Good Reason to develop a certain style.  My question, quite simply, 
>is: do you believe that there exist other Very Good Reasons, equally as 
>valid as yours, which might result in code that shows a style other than 
>yours?

Human nature unfortunately seems to have lots and lots of people who
won't or can't think this way.   Most wars today are fought by people
who have their Right way, therefore the other guys are Wrong and must
be punished.    Their belief that their way is right is a huge threat.

I suppose we all may have some of this (we are human).   I hope that I
am fighting such tendencies within myself.
```

```
On Sep 2, 1:19 pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
> In article <6c4db269-d581-42b0-a52a-750f41646...@t1g2000pra.googlegroups.com>,
>
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> procedure', Mr Plinston...

Your porous memory has failed you, yet again. It was you that
commented on 'changing indentation', to which I responded.

> what do you hope to gain by pointing out
> trivialities?

What do you hope to gain by tediously asking inane questions ?

> >Ensuring that the indentation
> >matches the logic levels is more important and also more arduous,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> *always* done it this way!'

It is nothing like that at all.

Interestingly, I have done very little 'always this way'.


> >> >And, for example, if the IF were later removed and the code
> >> >unindented, then presumably the 'dots everywhere' brigade would go
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> speak for themselves.

I'll wait until the 'some might', and then discuss it with them.

> >> >"""If you don't know what you are doing then do it neatly."""
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> sixty-degree one?

Neatness, or indeed most attributes, is also related to context.
Without context any opinion would be worthless.

>
> >> >Of course no code is ideal, some less so than others.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Your ideals may well be completely different from mine.

I have no particular interest in how long it takes you to maintain the
code that you are given to work with. I will judge, by my standards,
code that I am given.


> No matter what are ideals might be, Mr Plinston, if your assertion that
> 'no code is ideal' then neither yours nor mine is.

Some may be further away than others.


> >> >> Styles differ, Mr Plinston;
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Plinston... so you must, as well?  

It is certainly possible that I would make a statement as well.

> Please try to stay grounded.
>
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> yours?

There may well be other Reasons. Whether they are Very Good may well
depend on the context. Certainly the context of a site where your pay
cheque depends on certain styles make that a Very Good Reason. But I
don't work in that context.


> >> >That _may_ be because they haven't seen my code.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> appear to be generating a situation which does not exist.

Your point being ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-09-02T23:59:00+00:00

- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <6c4db269-d581-42b0-a52a-750f416462d8@t1g2000pra.googlegroups.com> <g9i4a8$e14$1@reader1.panix.com> <0b414a43-711f-4316-9984-c61094c1019b@n33g2000pri.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <0b414a43-711f-4316-9984-c61094c1019b@n33g2000pri.googlegroups.com>,
Richard  <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>On Sep 2, 1:19�pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:

>> The point I see you trying to make, Mr Plinston, is that you've found a
>> Very Good Reason to develop a certain style. �My question, quite simply,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>There may well be other Reasons.

I did not ask what there 'may be', Mr Plinston, I asked then, as I ask 
noq, for a second time: Do you believe that there exist other Very Good 
Reasons, equally as valid as yours, which might result in code that shows 
a style other than yours?

No subjunctives, optatives or conditionals, no need for elaboration, 
expansion or defense... just a statement of belief, a most fundamental 
'Credo'.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-09-02T23:19:28-05:00

- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <6c4db269-d581-42b0-a52a-750f416462d8@t1g2000pra.googlegroups.com> <g9i4a8$e14$1@reader1.panix.com> <0b414a43-711f-4316-9984-c61094c1019b@n33g2000pri.googlegroups.com> <g9kk04$rto$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Tue, 2 Sep 2008 23:59:00 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>In article <0b414a43-711f-4316-9984-c61094c1019b@n33g2000pri.googlegroups.com>,
>Richard  <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>a style other than yours?

The judgement criteria is simple: Quality. Even the worst among us knows the difference
between crap code and good code. You are defending crap code. 

>No subjunctives, optatives or conditionals, no need for elaboration, 
>expansion or defense... just a statement of belief, a most fundamental 
>'Credo'.

Coercion is the only rational reason to write crap code. The boss threatens to fire you.
That's very common in Cobol shops, especially those run by disenfranchised mainframers now
'stuck' with Unix machines.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-09-03T13:32:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g9m3l9$75c$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <jj3sb4l6sulvavvl3517ggir42dn70i9g4@4ax.com> <g9llqj$g6k$1@reader1.panix.com> <v31tb4111c8mn1hiupsa9tofg6qjlthgtu@4ax.com>`

```
In article <v31tb4111c8mn1hiupsa9tofg6qjlthgtu@4ax.com>,
Robert  <no@e.mail> wrote:
>On Wed, 3 Sep 2008 09:36:19 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:
>
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>Programming style is craft or art, not a moral or religious issue. 

Mr Wagner, as long as you hold that there are right and wrong ways to 
program (programming being a subset of 'anything than an organism does 
involving action and response to stimulation', a definition of 'behavior') 
- calling something 'crap code' might cause one to conclude that you do - 
and as long as 'moral' can be defined as 'of or relating to principles of 
right and wrong in behavior' then by your own language programming style 
is a moral issue.  If you don't like moral language then you might start 
by not using it.

Now, I noticed you failed to support your assertion above.  Second 
request, Mr Wagner: You assert that I am 'defending crap code'.  Please be 
so kind as to point out where my question above defends *any* kind of 
code... unless, of course, you are less interested in discussion and more 
interested in hearing people take issue with untruths you've stated about 
them.  I'll stand by what I've stated but I might not have much truck with 
what others people wish to believe that I have stated.

('I don't care if he did it... I just want to hear him deny it!')

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-09-03T12:33:23-07:00

```
> In article <v31tb4111c8mn1hiupsa9tofg6qjlth...@4ax.com>,
>
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
> by not using it.

I provided several _technical_ arguments that identified the code as
being poor, such as exceeding the array bounds, silently ignoring
errors, potentially exceeding stack limits. The program also did not
do what the specification claimed for it, even though it did this
adequately for the particular subset of data it was used for.

These were not 'style' issues, or moral, or religious. The program had
errors, some of which may have been the result of limitations of the
style used.


> Now, I noticed you failed to support your assertion above.  Second
> request, Mr Wagner: You assert that I am 'defending crap code'.  Please be
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> what others people wish to believe that I have stated.

And yet I feel much the same way when you assign me with 'control' or
'belief' or 'same as'.


> ('I don't care if he did it... I just want to hear him deny it!')
>
> DD
```

```
interested in something I will do my best to express my interest in a 
clear and unambiguous manner.  I do not say that I have any ability or 
inclination to control your words or when you say them... you, on the 
other hand, might considering attempting to do such in order to further a 
phenomenon sometimes called 'communication' (always a dicey thing to 
discuss, I know).

>
>If you feel that you are not interested then perhaps you could stop

might stop writing them... oh... that's right... you *can't*, because of

>

…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>fit.

Mr Plinston, or to yodel a mournful dirge in memory of your honored 
battle-dead... but such things have their time and place, as well.

A response of 'The refrigerator keeps the temperature quite constant!' to 
the question of 'believe that there exist other Very Good Reasons, equally 
as valid as yours, which might result in code that shows a style other 
than yours?' might demonstrate this 'freedom of speech'... and bring the 
world closer to one Ionesco portrayed, as well!... but it might make 
talking about code a bit more... challenging.

you've seen me do that a few times before.

suitable for twirling before I warn you about... the Mind-Control Rays!

will not be needed... and you can be sent off to serve (planetary 
overlords of your choice) in a more suitable manner.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-09-03T22:14:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3khub4lqquapq6p98js0al7m39c4ovj373@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <jj3sb4l6sulvavvl3517ggir42dn70i9g4@4ax.com> <g9llqj$g6k$1@reader1.panix.com> <v31tb4111c8mn1hiupsa9tofg6qjlthgtu@4ax.com> <g9m3l9$75c$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Wed, 3 Sep 2008 13:32:25 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>In article <v31tb4111c8mn1hiupsa9tofg6qjlthgtu@4ax.com>,
>Robert  <no@e.mail> wrote:
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
>by not using it.

The idea of right and wrong, good and bad, occurs in every aesthetic endeavor, as well as
religion,  ethics, morals and law. You equivocate when you argue that a single definition
or principle applies equally to all uses. 

Kant described the difference between a moral good (categorical) and a judgemental good
(hypothetical) by pointing out the latter must always appear under an IF clause, with the
single exception of good will. His critics say good will (ethical behavior toward others)
is not good because it is an obligation rather than an voluntary altruistic offering.
Economists say good is determined by the assessment of others, the marketplace. 
Other economists (utilitarians) say good is measured by happiness, in other words personal
preference. Logicians say good and bad are guesses at correct and incorrect based on
incomplete information. 

How do you reconcile those conflicting view into a single definition?

>Now, I noticed you failed to support your assertion above.  Second 
>request, Mr Wagner: You assert that I am 'defending crap code'.  Please be 
>so kind as to point out where my question above defends *any* kind of 
>code... 

You defended a programming style that pleases your timesheet signer. This is known as a
Nuremberg Defense. If you choose to abrogate personal responsibility then you have no
business discussing style issues, from either side. 

>unless, of course, you are less interested in discussion and more 
>interested in hearing people take issue with untruths you've stated about 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>('I don't care if he did it... I just want to hear him deny it!')

A good example is downthread, in an exchange about column 73.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest - GODWIN'D

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-09-04T23:14:28+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6i9u8mFpe0evU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <v31tb4111c8mn1hiupsa9tofg6qjlthgtu@4ax.com> <g9m3l9$75c$1@reader1.panix.com> <3khub4lqquapq6p98js0al7m39c4ovj373@4ax.com> <g9o1j5$jh8$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```


<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:g9o1j5$jh8$1@reader1.panix.com...
> In article <3khub4lqquapq6p98js0al7m39c4ovj373@4ax.com>,
> Robert  <no@e.mail> wrote:
…[98 more quoted lines elided]…
> same manner as Mr Dashwood... are you working towards Management?

You really are pathetic here, Mr.Dwarf.

First off Godwin's law only applies to people who accept it. I don't. A 
thread can continue perfectly well whether or not mention of Nazis is made, 
especially when such mention is pertinent to the matter under discussion. 
YOU may have decided you "won" a debate by citing it; what is far more 
likely is that I simply stopped wasting my time responding to you. (I 
actually can't even be bothered looking up the case in point...)

(Certainly this thread is stupid and pointless and could profitably be 
stopped, but that has nothing to do with Godwin's law...)

Secondly, a Nuremburg defense (real or imagined) has nothing to do with 
Godwin's law either. It is perfectly legitimate (and a verifiable fact) that 
certain criminals tried in Nuremburg for War Crimes after WWII cited as a 
defence that they " were only following orders".

Saying: "I do whatever pleases the person who signs my timesheet" has a 
certain undeniable similarity. Should such person require you to cut off a 
colleague's finger, for example, how can we know that you wouldn't? It 
starts with providing bad code because you were asked to... who knows where 
it ends?

As for working towards Management, you really shouldn't discuss matters you 
have absolutely no conception or understanding of.

Your posts here reveal a consistently hostile attitude towards the people 
who manage you and you even used the word as a term of abuse to me on one 
occasion. (it actually made me smile).

You see only bad managers, when in fact there are good and bad managers, 
just as there are good and bad programmers, and good and bad people.

However there is only one Doc Dwarf... :-)

Pete.
```

```
On Thu, 4 Sep 2008 07:09:26 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>In article <3khub4lqquapq6p98js0al7m39c4ovj373@4ax.com>,
>Robert  <no@e.mail> wrote:

I did, when I said "The judgement criteria is simple: Quality." Quality limits the scope
of "good" to excellence, subjective judgement, rather than political, moral or religious
'truth'.

>>How do you reconcile those conflicting view into a single definition?
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>I've done this.

You defend crap code by changing the definition of "good" to a political one. You say good
code is whatever makes the boss happy, and bad code is what makes him unhappy. If the boss
wants fall through paragraphs and GO TOs, you think the resulting code is good, under
those conditions. I disagree.

We're supposed to be professionals, not code monkeys.

>>>Now, I noticed you failed to support your assertion above.  Second 
>>>request, Mr Wagner: You assert that I am 'defending crap code'.  Please be 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>same manner as Mr Dashwood... are you working towards Management?  

I'm working away from management. I started my career as a manager, at age 23, then worked
my way down to programmer. 

>A bit of research iu the Jargon File should show you how a comparison 
>involving Hitler or Nazis can change participation in the thread.

Lt. William Calley, leader of the My Lai Massacre, invoked a Nuremberg Defense. He was not
accused of being a Nazi, just a person shirking responsibility for his actions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest - GODWIN'D

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2008-09-04T12:25:01-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1f90c4dsnqufbj82pe5jsrd8gr2u2tikpf@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <v31tb4111c8mn1hiupsa9tofg6qjlthgtu@4ax.com> <g9m3l9$75c$1@reader1.panix.com> <3khub4lqquapq6p98js0al7m39c4ovj373@4ax.com> <g9o1j5$jh8$1@reader1.panix.com> <v050c4l6bhhe1b8cv9f6c7pdvqbn1vagej@4ax.com>`

```
On Thu, 04 Sep 2008 12:40:33 -0500, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:

>You defend crap code by changing the definition of "good" to a political one. You say good
>code is whatever makes the boss happy, and bad code is what makes him unhappy. If the boss
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>We're supposed to be professionals, not code monkeys.

Quality gets measured by how well the product accomplishes the goals -
which are the wants and needs of those who pay for the product. (Which
usually include various levels of bosses)

If my boss asks me to create a rope bridge, and I provide a beautiful
concrete bridge - my boss is the one who decides whether I produced a
quality product or not.   (It depends on his goals).

We all have rules that we've got to follow - even though we disagree
with them.   Doing things "better" than what these rules demand may
impact our ability to work adversely.    Sometimes the law is an ass.
But we've got to learn to ride that ass instead of taking the horse we
prefer.

If we're capable enough, we find a way to deliver a quality product
that works for our boss, our users, and for ourselves.   That requires
some flexibility.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest - GODWIN'D

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest - GODWIN'D

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-09-04T20:33:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ksr0c4lomk62vpkbpjkvlo6h1qnrild13q@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <v31tb4111c8mn1hiupsa9tofg6qjlthgtu@4ax.com> <g9m3l9$75c$1@reader1.panix.com> <3khub4lqquapq6p98js0al7m39c4ovj373@4ax.com> <g9o1j5$jh8$1@reader1.panix.com> <v050c4l6bhhe1b8cv9f6c7pdvqbn1vagej@4ax.com> <1f90c4dsnqufbj82pe5jsrd8gr2u2tikpf@4ax.com>`

```
On Thu, 04 Sep 2008 12:25:01 -0600, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>On Thu, 04 Sep 2008 12:40:33 -0500, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>quality product or not.   (It depends on his goals).

The boss wants a rope bridge made with granny knots, and I provide a better, stronger one
made with sugeon's knots and carrick bends. The boss says time stopped when he tied his
last knot in 1980, so I can't use any knot developed in the last 28 years. If users see
this bridge, they'll all want one. Others say the design standard should be based on the
2am maintenance rigger. 

For the last twenty years, user demand for rope bridges has been declining because
cable-stayed footbridges made from prefabricated fiber-reinforced polymer can be erected
four times faster and for one tenth the cost. India tried building rope bridges for
awhile, but have since switched to newer technologies. The only rope builders left are old
timers, who are often seen refighting a war that ended more than twenty years ago.
```

```
In that case, I will disagree with you.   There are often multiple
True Ways that are valid and work.    Some paths are obviously less
optimized for my needs, tastes, and wants than others, but if my buddy
chooses one of them, and gets to work on time - that path isn't wrong.
```

- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <6c4db269-d581-42b0-a52a-750f416462d8@t1g2000pra.googlegroups.com> <g9i4a8$e14$1@reader1.panix.com> <0b414a43-711f-4316-9984-c61094c1019b@n33g2000pri.googlegroups.com> <g9kk04$rto$1@reader1.panix.com> <jj3sb4l6sulvavvl3517ggir42dn70i9g4@4ax.com> <ad4tb4ple6peloh6he01e26qkfqmt1boga@4ax.com>`

```
On Wed, 03 Sep 2008 07:34:00 -0600, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>On Tue, 02 Sep 2008 23:19:28 -0500, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>I saw DD arguing against there being only one True Way.  

The most dogmatic people are proponents of crap code. They formalize their rules as Shop
Standards and insist others follow them to the letter, going so far as to fire
disbelievers. They are the ones who believe in one True Way, while pretending to be
agnostic on style. Their message is 'there is no such thing as bad programming, so do it
my way.'

Their opposites have opinions about good and bad practices, but do not force those
opinions on others with such zeal and rigidity.
```

- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <0b414a43-711f-4316-9984-c61094c1019b@n33g2000pri.googlegroups.com> <g9kk04$rto$1@reader1.panix.com> <960ad6e7-ad46-44d5-9d18-9d738b6981b5@r35g2000prm.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <960ad6e7-ad46-44d5-9d18-9d738b6981b5@r35g2000prm.googlegroups.com>,
Richard  <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>On Sep 3, 11:59�am, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
>> In article
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>have already answered, and you simply clipped that.

Mr Plinston, you answered this question with a statement beginning with 
'There may be' and I do not recall your making any statement which 
included the words 'I believe'; since the question was of your beliefs and 
not of the activities of others you did not answer.  
<http://groups.google.com/group/comp.lang.cobol/msg/6f2f94df883e938d?dmode=source> 
seems to bear that out.

But you know me well enough by now, and the audience knows you well enough 
by now, and you'll be able to protect yourself from instrospection or 
revelation by a simple step-aside... which might, due to a lack of 
introspection, ignore the amount of revelation it contains.

Third and final time, Mr Plinston: Do you believe that there exist other 
Very Good Reasons, equally as valid as yours, which might result in code 
that shows a style other than yours?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-09-03T11:50:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d8c2f355-f77b-4022-b7a2-c25e80e02703@x16g2000prn.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <0b414a43-711f-4316-9984-c61094c1019b@n33g2000pri.googlegroups.com> <g9kk04$rto$1@reader1.panix.com> <960ad6e7-ad46-44d5-9d18-9d738b6981b5@r35g2000prm.googlegroups.com> <g9lmi3$p0p$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Sep 3, 9:48 pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
> In article <960ad6e7-ad46-44d5-9d18-9d738b698...@r35g2000prm.googlegroups.com>,
>
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> included the words 'I believe';

I don't have 'beliefs'. I avoid using the word 'believe' because it is
often misunderstood. If I have evidence then I evaluate it. If you
don't like that then sod off and stop attempting to control my words.


> since the question was of your beliefs and
> not of the activities of others you did not answer.  
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> introspection, ignore the amount of revelation it contains.

"Introspection", "revelation", my goodness, a right little amateur
psychologist you are now. Of course you presume that I don't think
about such things, and then arrogantly conclude that I if I did I
would come to your conclusion.

If you actually have a case to make then do so. It may be less tedious
than your usual diatribe.


> Third and final time,

Well we wouldn't want you appear obsessive about this would we.

> Mr Plinston: Do you believe that there exist other
> Very Good Reasons, equally as valid as yours, which might result in code
> that shows a style other than yours?

Given that you are very precise about your use of English, then the
capitalising of 'Very Good Reasons' makes this a proper noun-phrase.
Just as 'Main Road' need not be a main road then I no no ability to
comment on what you are referring to without you showing the content.

Given that people with obsessive-compulsive behaviour may make lists
of what they think of as 'Very Good Reasons', then yes, these may
exist.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-09-03T19:23:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g9mo8c$q6l$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <960ad6e7-ad46-44d5-9d18-9d738b6981b5@r35g2000prm.googlegroups.com> <g9lmi3$p0p$1@reader1.panix.com> <d8c2f355-f77b-4022-b7a2-c25e80e02703@x16g2000prn.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <d8c2f355-f77b-4022-b7a2-c25e80e02703@x16g2000prn.googlegroups.com>,
Richard  <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>On Sep 3, 9:48�pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
>> In article
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>I don't have 'beliefs'.

Mr Plinston, I have no idea what, to you, is the difference between a 
belief and a 'belief'.

>I avoid using the word 'believe' because it is
>often misunderstood.

I have found that conversation in good form - starting with definitions, 
postulates and common notions before beginning with attempts at 
demonstrations - and done in good faith has a tendency to decrease 
misunderstandings.  You might try doing so, yourself, and seeing if the 
results are similar.

>If I have evidence then I evaluate it. If you
>don't like that then sod off and stop attempting to control my words.

I am not attempting to control your words, Mr Plinston, I am asking a 
question about what you believe.

[snip]

>Given that people with obsessive-compulsive behaviour may make lists
>of what they think of as 'Very Good Reasons', then yes, these may
>exist.

Mr Plinston, if you are saying that a pathology prevents you from 
participating in good form and good faith then you certainly have taken a 
long way around to get there.

If you're saying otherwise... perhaps you might re-state yourself... or 
read back a few sentences and consider the possibility of following your 
own advice.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-09-03T13:35:50-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fb49c7d0-f5b5-435c-a159-69c082e90353@v16g2000prc.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <960ad6e7-ad46-44d5-9d18-9d738b6981b5@r35g2000prm.googlegroups.com> <g9lmi3$p0p$1@reader1.panix.com> <d8c2f355-f77b-4022-b7a2-c25e80e02703@x16g2000prn.googlegroups.com> <g9mo8c$q6l$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Sep 4, 7:23 am, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
> In article <d8c2f355-f77b-4022-b7a2-c25e80e02...@x16g2000prn.googlegroups.com>,
>
…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
> I am not attempting to control your words, Mr Plinston,

In what way is:

"""No subjunctives, optatives or conditionals, no need for
elaboration, expansion or defense... """

_NOT_ attempted control on my words ?

You need to do some introspection yourself.


>  I am asking a
> question about what you believe.

Which I have responded to.

I have indicated what I use and what I prefer.

I have also stated that I have no particular interest in whether
others use whatever style they are comfortable with, as long as they
don't expect me to work on the code.

I have, over the last 40 years, used many styles and several different
languages. This has allowed me to see advantages in particular
techniques that someone who only wrote Cobol may not consider, or even
notice.

Your claim, for example, was that to restrict a paragraph to a single
'idea' (which may be different from mine) was a 'limitation'. I can
imagine styles in which that could be true, in fact I probably used
such styles in the past. However, it is not a limitation in my current
style.

You are obviously attempting to show that I am preaching 'One True
Way'. I am not. I have progressed through several styles, some of
which you may still be using and may have very good reasons for doing
so, but I had reasons for changing and may very well continue to
change, but not backwards.

If you have any 'Very Good Reasons' that are progressive then you
should state them and then I could evaluate them to see if I can use
them.


> [snip]
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> participating in good form and good faith

You seem to think that 'good form and good faith' is done by using the
words that you want to put in other's mouths.

> then you certainly have taken a
> long way around to get there.

Getting nowhere seems to be a feature of the threads that you
participate in. Have you introspected to see why this might be ?

> If you're saying otherwise... perhaps you might re-state yourself...

It seems to be particularly pointless to do so, or has been each time
that I have, you simply clip what I say and claim it was no answer at
all.

> or
> read back a few sentences and consider the possibility of following your
> own advice.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-09-04T00:37:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g9najr$s6f$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <d8c2f355-f77b-4022-b7a2-c25e80e02703@x16g2000prn.googlegroups.com> <g9mo8c$q6l$1@reader1.panix.com> <fb49c7d0-f5b5-435c-a159-69c082e90353@v16g2000prc.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <fb49c7d0-f5b5-435c-a159-69c082e90353@v16g2000prc.googlegroups.com>,
Richard  <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>On Sep 4, 7:23�am, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
>> In article
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Richard �<rip...@azonic.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>> >I don't have 'beliefs'.
>>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>_NOT_ attempted control on my words ?

Mr Plinston, the fragment you quote appears to be removed from its 
context.  If you are citing 
<http://groups.google.com/group/comp.lang.cobol/msg/5b5432efb1102809?dmode=source> 
you will see the following:

--begin quoted text

I did not ask what there 'may be', Mr Plinston, I asked then, as I ask 
noq, for a second time: Do you believe that there exist other Very Good 
Reasons, equally as valid as yours, which might result in code that shows 
a style other than yours?

No subjunctives, optatives or conditionals, no need for elaboration, 
expansion or defense... just a statement of belief, a most fundamental 
'Credo'.

--end quoted text

... and in this form 'No subjunctives (etc)' is a continuation of the 
sentence beginning with 'I did not ask' preceding.  Just as a common form 
is 'I looked out the window.  It is raining.' is, quite often, a 
contraction of 'I looked out the window.  What I saw out the window causes 
me to conclude that it is raining' so my intended form was 'I did not ask 
what there 'may be'... I asked for no subjunctives (etc), there is no need 
for elaboration (etc), I asked for just a statement (etc)'.

That is how it was intended, Mr Plinston... that you saw it otherwise 
speaks of your interpretation, nothing more.

>
>You need to do some introspection yourself.

Perhaps I might be a bit more specific in certain things, Mr Plinston... 
but in a quest for elegance I might have erred on the side of 
miscomprehensibility.

>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Which I have responded to.

In what might be seen as a rather revealing manner, as well.

[snip]

>Your claim, for example, was that to restrict a paragraph to a single
>'idea' (which may be different from mine) was a 'limitation'. I can
>imagine styles in which that could be true, in fact I probably used
>such styles in the past. However, it is not a limitation in my current
>style.

There is no need to imagine, Mr Plinston, that 'to limit' is 'to restrict 
the bounds of'.  Are you saying now that when you converse what you 
imagine might be should be considered before simple definitions from a 
commonly-accepted source?

>
>You are obviously attempting to show that I am preaching 'One True
>Way'.

At least one person saw something entirely different in what I was 
attempting to do, Mr Plinston... and I came closer to agreeing with his 
evaluation of my attempts than I have with yours.  What might you be able 
to imagine from this?

[snip]

>> Mr Plinston, if you are saying that a pathology prevents you from
>> participating in good form and good faith
>
>You seem to think that 'good form and good faith' is done by using the
>words that you want to put in other's mouths.

Mr Plinston, I stated, clearly and unambiguously, in 
<http://groups.google.com/group/comp.lang.cobol/msg/b19e993585c9da84?dmode=source> 
what I find good form to be... as for 'good faith', I'll take 'absence of 
any intent to defraud, act maliciously or take unfair advantage of'.  If 
you conclude, for whatever reasons, that I am not doing this then you 
might find yourself happier were you to... do something that would make 
you happier, I am not sure what it is.

DD
```

```
In article <998a67e5-0231-49ae-bb0f-b7b2c6e1d99b@v16g2000prc.googlegroups.com>,
Richard  <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>> In article
…[69 more quoted lines elided]…
>difference from what you intended is the fault of the reader ?

No, Mr Plinston, my claim was that your question of 'In what way is:
"""No subjunctives, optatives or conditionals, no need for
elaboration, expansion or defense... """
_NOT_ attempted control on my words ?' was intended otherwise.  I gave the 
quotations context and explained my use of the language.  If you wish to 
call arriving at a different conclusion in the absence of the 
clarification an assignment of 'fault' then there's nothing in English 
that prevents you from doing so.

>You
>then require _others_ to do introspection.

I require others to do... *something*, Mr Plinston... conversation and 
discussion are rather difficult without it.

>
>In fact you have been arguing that a paragraph contains one (or more)
>ideas. The "No subjunctives ..." is indeed a new paragraph, so how do
>you claim now that it is merely a continuation from the previous
>paragraph.

By the explanation given above, Mr Plinston... if you need it repeated 
then it is easy enough to re-read.

>And why should I think that it is a continuation (ignoring
>the complete disjoint of the new paragraph) of the first phrase when
>the usual convention is to continue the immediate prior phrase of "I
>ask now".

Because the author of the paragraph, when it was pointed out that his 
intent to convey this sense had failed, explained it to be such... is this 
*really* that difficult?

>
>Your "No subjunctives .." is indeed given as a qualifier and
>constraint on what my answer must, or must not be.

Mr Plinston, I have just explained, as author of the words in question, 
what style I was attempting to use and what forms I was attempting to 
shape the words towards.  It just might, vaguely, be possible that someone 
other than the author would, in fct, know better than the author what said 
author was attempting to convey... but this does not appear to be one of 
those situations.

>
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>successful). Now I see that this was a mere by-product.

My apologies if you experience tedium, Mr Plinston... perhaps you'd prefer 
to discuss something else with someone else, as little stops you... or 
perhaps you might gaze out upon a sea of twinkling lights, as well.

[snip]

>> >Your claim, for example, was that to restrict a paragraph to a single
>> >'idea' (which may be different from mine) was a 'limitation'. I can
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Was there a point where I said that ?

There was a point, immediately preceding, where it proceeded logically 
from your statements, Mr Plinston:

'You said Your claim, for example, was that to restrict a paragraph to a 
single 'idea' (which may be different from mine) was a 'limitation'.  I 
can imagine styles in which that could be true, in fact I probably used 
such styles in the past.'

'There is no need to imagine, Mr Plinston, that 'to limit' is 'to 
restrict the bounds of'.

http://dictionary.reference.com/browse/limit, 7:to restrict by or as if by 
establishing limits.

Whether you, personally, do it or not, Mr Plinston, to restrict the number 
of sentences you place in a paragraph is to restrict the bounds of the 
number of sentences you place in a paragraph.  This is one of the ways 
that English works.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-09-02T13:28:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<49f3a538-3b86-473b-8a31-12bd73618088@s20g2000prd.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <92fcce4e-2525-4bc8-a4ef-aeff6ac7a50a@n33g2000pri.googlegroups.com> <g9fd5c$min$1@reader1.panix.com> <88af676a-5ab8-44cb-a9ac-b06fb0f76e12@w1g2000prk.googlegroups.com> <g9gvu1$31h$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Sep 2, 2:58 am, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:

> >> I was taugh, e'er-so-long ago, that the
> >> intention of COBOL was to be English-like to the point where a period/full
> >> stop was to signal the end of a sentence, the completion of a thought, the
> >> full conveying of a particular set of instructions.

You are limiting the 'English-like' to only be prose. In prose the
punctuation is added to the words so that when the words are
vocalised, either internally or out loud, the pauses and inflections
which are used in speech may be added correctly. They ensure that
correct meaning is transferred.

Other forms of writings do not use punctuation in the same way. A
recipe may omit all punctuation in the ingredients, or even in the
instructions. The meaning of the words is indicated entirely by the
way they are listed on separate lines. No inflections or pauses are
required.

Poetry can be written without punctuation, or at least less than prose
would have. The words are organised into lines and stanzas and the
rhythm and inflections are added by conventions.

Other lists or instruction can similarly be unpunctuated, the
separation of 'ideas' or steps indicated by line feeds and grouped by
indentation.

Program code is not prose. It usually does not run on, filling the
lines and pages aligned left with just a space between each word and
after punctuation. It is not word wrapped or justified, or hyphenated,
though it can be (sort of).

If you did write your code in a manner like prose then it may be
necessary to use punctuation as a guide to the start and end of each
statement and clause.

However, as I was commenting on _POINTLESS_ full stops and useless
commas (they are ignored by the compiler and serve no purpose) then I
leave it to each to examine whether the ones that they use have a
point to them or not.


> >Yes, but even you have specified the plural 'instructions'.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> style.  

It is not a limitation unless it is difficult to fit into the
structure of the program.

For example if every pargraph _must_ be given a number and then the
code ordered by these so that they can easily be found in the pack of
cards, then yes, having shorter paragraphs that are based on one
'idea' or doing just one thing may seem to be a limitation.

It may also be that with PERFORM THRU or PERFORM SECTION it becomes
too hard to arrange the code usefully in smaller and more manageable
blocks.

However, the program organization that I use lends itself to easily
break down the code into reusable parts. If there is any 'limitation'
it is not mine, but that of the other program organization used.


> A paragraph, by definition, contains 'one or more sentences'; by
> requiring yourself to keep to a subset of the definition ('one sentence
> only') you practise and advocate a subset of possibilities.

It is what I practice, yes. Where did you see the 'advocate' ?


> Your reasons for doing this are your own, of course... but to call
> something other than the particular subset you've chosen 'idiotic' does
> not, to me, appear to be a reasoned or dispassioned evaluation.
```

```
wrote:

>You are limiting the 'English-like' to only be prose. In prose the
>punctuation is added to the words so that when the words are
>vocalised, either internally or out loud, the pauses and inflections
>which are used in speech may be added correctly. They ensure that
>correct meaning is transferred.
```

```
72. If it falls in card column 73, it is a source of merriment to the gods of confusion.
```

```
In article <o94sb41ti8ngprd262v0385pju6t95f838@4ax.com>,
Robert  <no@e.mail> wrote:

>>No, Mr Plinston, I am limiting 'English-like' to 'the point where a 
>>period/full stop was to signal the end of a sentence, the completion of a 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>gods of confusion.

Well, so much for the promise of free-form code, Mr Wagner... you'd best 
rush back to prefacing your paragraph-names with numbers in case the 
card-deck drops.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-09-03T22:14:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gmgub4honm1mq4et6fd2tvi6pe6k39mr9q@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <o94sb41ti8ngprd262v0385pju6t95f838@4ax.com> <g9lmlj$8q0$1@reader1.panix.com> <rb2tb49j5u1tm8snfdeudim95gg6ill8dl@4ax.com> <g9m3pg$ri6$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Wed, 3 Sep 2008 13:34:40 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>In article <rb2tb49j5u1tm8snfdeudim95gg6ill8dl@4ax.com>,
>Robert  <no@e.mail> wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>merriment to the gods of confusion'?

I said a disadvantage of fixed form is that a period in column 73 does not signal the end
of a sentence. You said that proves free form does not solve the problem, when in fact it
does. 

I clarified by saying a period in column 73 DOES function as intended under free form. You
misrepresented my "merriment" comment as a reference to free form, when in fact it was a
reference to fixed form.

Twice in one thread, you've twisted my words to mean something different from what I said.
I don't believe they were accidental misunderstandings; I think you did it deliberately.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-09-04T07:46:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
In article <gmgub4honm1mq4et6fd2tvi6pe6k39mr9q@4ax.com>,
Robert  <no@e.mail> wrote:
>On Wed, 3 Sep 2008 13:34:40 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:
>
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>does. 

I believe you have our positions reversed, Mr Wagner.  In 
Mhttp://groups.google.com/group/comp.lang.cobol/msg/5a8b5f80f64ccaec?dmode=source> 
you wrote:

--begin quoted text:

A period/full stop only signals the end of sentence if it falls between 
card columns 8 and 72. If it falls in card column 73, it is a source of 
merriment to the gods of confusion.

--end quoted text

... and this seems to be valid only under the rules of fixed-form text.  
In 
<http://groups.google.com/group/comp.lang.cobol/msg/3c906ad5c75cbc9b?dmode=source> 
I quoted your posting in full, appending

--begin quoted text:

Well, so much for the promise of free-form code, Mr Wagner... you'd best 
rush back to prefacing your paragraph-names with numbers in case the 
card-deck drops.

--end quoted text

... which was intended to indicate your earlier assertion did not hold 
under the free-form text rules.

[snip]

>Twice in one thread, you've twisted my words to mean something different
>from what I said.
>I don't believe they were accidental misunderstandings; I think you did
>it deliberately. 

Mr Wagner, more times than once, in many threads, I've quoted your words 
exactly and added a sentence which appears to indicate the conclusion you 
posted was not the One True Conclusion which might be reached.  If that's 
'twuisting your words to mean something different' then you might consider 
using language a bit less... elastic.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2008-09-03T07:39:34-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cp4tb45egjs00m9ph5suaa7q38jq1cnhno@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <88af676a-5ab8-44cb-a9ac-b06fb0f76e12@w1g2000prk.googlegroups.com> <g9gvu1$31h$1@reader1.panix.com> <49f3a538-3b86-473b-8a31-12bd73618088@s20g2000prd.googlegroups.com> <g9kkkd$gu2$1@reader1.panix.com> <o94sb41ti8ngprd262v0385pju6t95f838@4ax.com>`

```
On Tue, 02 Sep 2008 23:28:28 -0500, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:

>A period/full stop only signals the end of sentence if it falls between card columns 8 and
>72. If it falls in card column 73, it is a source of merriment to the gods of confusion.

An end-if only signals the end of an if clause if it falls between
card columns 8 and 72 (with most compilers).   If it is written in
columns 73-78, it is a source of merriment to the gods of confusion.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-09-03T10:04:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g9lneo$74r$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <49f3a538-3b86-473b-8a31-12bd73618088@s20g2000prd.googlegroups.com> <g9kkkd$gu2$1@reader1.panix.com> <aa14f7fc-1896-4aa4-873d-7c9c439458cf@1g2000pre.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <aa14f7fc-1896-4aa4-873d-7c9c439458cf@1g2000pre.googlegroups.com>,
Richard  <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>On Sep 3, 12:09�pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
>> In article
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>Which relates to prose.

'Relates to' does not, as I understand the language, equate to 'is 
completely limited to'.

>Poems, for example are usually arranged in
>stanzas. Lists, signs, instructions, and others need not be sentences.

Mr Plinston, I'm not sure what dictionary you are using... but according 
to http://www.m-w.com the -like suffix is defined as 'resembling or having 
the characteristics of'.  Your reasons for confusing it with 'being the 
same as or meeting all criteria of' (or something similar) are not clear.

>
>In any case I prefer my programs to be code-like regardless of the
>language they are written in.

That you prefer this, Mr Plinston, in no wise eliminates the intention of 
COBOL's design to be English-like... you *did* know that was part of the 
language's design documents, didn't you?

>
>But why do you put several different ideas into one paragraph ?

I would do so in order to attempt to have a paragraph contain several 
different ideas, Mr Plinston; I did not realise that possibility needed 
explanation.

>This
>must reduce the re-usability of them ? Or is this not a criteria by
>which you write code ?

I do not now, nor can I recall doing, nor can I see myself writing code in 
the future, Mr Plinston, which is based on the criterion that every 
paragraph must be re-usable in all places at all times, no, as the more 
specific code is to a situation the less re-useable it is... unless you 
believe that a paragraph which calculates compound interest must be 
re-useable as one which formats a report-line you might come to a similar 
conclusion.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-09-03T12:22:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d329792-d8bf-46d6-b3aa-e3dc93806c99@s1g2000pra.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <49f3a538-3b86-473b-8a31-12bd73618088@s20g2000prd.googlegroups.com> <g9kkkd$gu2$1@reader1.panix.com> <aa14f7fc-1896-4aa4-873d-7c9c439458cf@1g2000pre.googlegroups.com> <g9lneo$74r$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Sep 3, 10:04 pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
> In article <aa14f7fc-1896-4aa4-873d-7c9c43945...@1g2000pre.googlegroups.com>,
>
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> same as or meeting all criteria of' (or something similar) are not clear.

Where did I say it was 'the same or meeting all criteria of'

I am not confusing it at all. Cobol can be English-like without being
Prose-like.


> >In any case I prefer my programs to be code-like regardless of the
> >language they are written in.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> language's design documents, didn't you?

Not having read those particular documents myself I can only rely on
reports that this was the case.

Whether it actually achieves this is moot, the language has its own
set of rules and does not use any of the dictionary references that
have provided. The full-stop does not provide the same functionality
that is present in written English. It's presence is sometimes
required at specific points as a terminator and may be used at some
other points as, essentially, decoration.


> >But why do you put several different ideas into one paragraph ?
>
> I would do so in order to attempt to have a paragraph contain several
> different ideas, Mr Plinston; I did not realise that possibility needed
> explanation.

And you don't see any disadvantage in having such mixtures ?

I think that it may be difficult for you to adapt to OO.

> >This
> >must reduce the re-usability of them ? Or is this not a criteria by
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> conclusion.

Surely 'formatting a report-line' is just one idea.

Now, in fact, I do much of my programming in Python these days. The
'format a report line' is done in my code using a template and the
data in a list or dictionary. The template is a text file, or
postscript, or such and the output is print line or html or xml or
whatever the user requires, the program doesn't much care what it
actually looks like.

So, yes, I very much do think that code that 'formats a report-line'
is re-usable everywhere.

I also do templating in Cobol for generating HTML and PostScript
output, Most reports from my systems come from a report generator
which processes, selects, sorts and formats the output based on a
script which directs this.

So, yes, I very do think that code that 'formats a report-line' is re-
usable everywhere.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-09-04T01:59:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eb4faef5-d95f-4c79-a0b1-077fee9bd561@w24g2000prd.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <4e8981e0-436c-4ded-8bc0-00b876c13df3@s9g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <g9narl$ruq$1@reader1.panix.com> <ad5b83f0-8084-40b7-8985-485b48ae6798@a3g2000prm.googlegroups.com> <g9o44m$ipf$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Sep 4, 7:52 pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
> In article <ad5b83f0-8084-40b7-8985-485b48ae6...@a3g2000prm.googlegroups.com>,
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> language and its form.

Do you not think it more useful to study the _current_ intention of
the language and its form ? or at least an intermediary that is less
than half a century old.

I was well aware of the 'intent and form' of the Cobol'61 that I
originally used, and of the ANS'74 and ANS'85 and I recognised the
changing 'intent and form' that resulted, and also that of the 2002
standard with its addition of Object Orientation.

Perhaps you are unhappy with the progress that the language has made
and wish it to have remained as you initially were taught it.


> >Perhaps if you start by explaining why you would want to revert to, or
> >remain in, the world of the 1950s where computers were regarded as
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> consider your needs and wishes to expand your horizons more.

I do so, continuously.

In the 60s I learnt what the 60s offered, in the 70s and afterwards I
learned new things as they developed. I still do so.

I ask again (and may ask a third time): Please explain why you would
want to revert to, or remain in, the world of the 1950s.


> I am not about to say anything stronger than I have... perhaps this will
> avoid your making an accusation of my trying to twist your words, shape
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD
```

```
question.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Robin Lee <robinlee@news.com>
- **Date:** 2008-09-02T17:14:53-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2--dnaBwBeaDMCDVnZ2dnUVZ_jKdnZ2d@giganews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<88af676a-5ab8-44cb-a9ac-b06fb0f76e12@w1g2000prk.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <92fcce4e-2525-4bc8-a4ef-aeff6ac7a50a@n33g2000pri.googlegroups.com> <g9fd5c$min$1@reader1.panix.com> <88af676a-5ab8-44cb-a9ac-b06fb0f76e12@w1g2000prk.googlegroups.com>`

```
> 
>>> ... I think that
>>> scattering pointless full stops, and especially the useless commas,
>>> through the code as idiotic.
>

Admittedly without having read very single post in this thread, I have to pitch in 
my two cents regarding punctuation. Personally I too believe that in most cases, 
punctuation should be reserved for those places in the code where it is necessary.

Two cases come immediately to mind, both from lessons painfully learned as a 
"maintainer", and both involve situations which required stripping extraneous 
punctuation from code.

One situation is when (as has probably already been mentioned) straight line code is 
modified to be sandwiched within conditionals, in cases where it would be confusing 
or dangerous to relocate the entire chunk of code to some other paragraph.

E.G.:
	PERFORM PARA-ONE.
	PERFORM PARA-TWO.
	PERFORM PARA-THREE.

becomes:
	IF CONDITION
		PERFORM PARA-ONE
		PERFORM PARA-TWO
		PERFORM PARA-THREE.

... requires removing most of the totally unnecessary periods from each line.

But the one that really gets me steamed is those who insist on terminating each line 
with a comma. When reading thru the code, a comma can easily appear to be a period, 
and more than a few times I've been burned when a procedure appeared to be 
terminated by a period that in fact (after much troubleshooting) turned out instead 
to be a comma.

	PERFORM PARA-ONE,
	PERFORM PARA-TWO,
	PERFORM PARA-THREE.

It seems obvious in this brief example above, but in a large program, when EVERY 
line of code has a dot at the end, they all tend to look alike.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-09-03T00:33:57-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<t08sb4prsl5sf07ocm4stk5cpo8qe42l04@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <92fcce4e-2525-4bc8-a4ef-aeff6ac7a50a@n33g2000pri.googlegroups.com> <g9fd5c$min$1@reader1.panix.com> <88af676a-5ab8-44cb-a9ac-b06fb0f76e12@w1g2000prk.googlegroups.com> <2--dnaBwBeaDMCDVnZ2dnUVZ_jKdnZ2d@giganews.com>`

```
On Tue, 02 Sep 2008 17:14:53 -0400, Robin Lee <robinlee@news.com> wrote:

>> 
>>>> ... I think that
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>... requires removing most of the totally unnecessary periods from each line.

All periods are unnecessary. The IF should be terminated by END-IF. 

Suppose you want to write

if condition
   if condition
      perform para-one
  end-if
  perform para-two
  perform para-three
end-if

You cannot do it with the 'period model'.

Cobol requires a period on the last sentence of a paragraph. The workaround is simple: put
a period before every paragraph name. See cobdata for an example.

>But the one that really gets me steamed is those who insist on terminating each line 
>with a comma. When reading thru the code, a comma can easily appear to be a period, 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>	PERFORM PARA-THREE.

One editor command can remove the commas.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Contest

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2008-09-03T07:45:53-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<315tb41hkkrs8j5ibv252ruikr5r4pjqir@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<126a9c5f-3de2-4271-9cee-d9fabdaf26b4@i76g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <92fcce4e-2525-4bc8-a4ef-aeff6ac7a50a@n33g2000pri.googlegroups.com> <g9fd5c$min$1@reader1.panix.com> <88af676a-5ab8-44cb-a9ac-b06fb0f76e12@w1g2000prk.googlegroups.com> <2--dnaBwBeaDMCDVnZ2dnUVZ_jKdnZ2d@giganews.com>`

```
On Tue, 02 Sep 2008 17:14:53 -0400, Robin Lee <robinlee@news.com>
wrote:

>	PERFORM PARA-ONE.
>	PERFORM PARA-TWO.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>... requires removing most of the totally unnecessary periods from each line.

My choice here is to wrap the code with either an END-IF or as a
separate paragraph.   The compiler will let me know if I was careless
with my review and indention of the code between the IF and END-IF.

And moving the data to a separate paragraph works fine.

If you are an advocate of one period per paragraph, then I extremely
doubt you would add an IF to code without an END-IF.
```

```
On Sun, 31 Aug 2008 13:08:14 -0700 (PDT), Richard
<riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Your paragraph CC does have one additional full stop. The questions
>then are: does it add anything to the code ? (no, not at all) and:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

The easiest and safest way to do this is to create a new paragraph,
with the conditional pointing to it.

I wouldn't want to put a conditional around code without examining the
code anyway.

A period is a guarantee of a full-stop, even with inserted
pre-compiler and copy member code.
```
