###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Declining Cobol job market

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "klshafer@att.net" <klshafer@att.net>
- **Date:** 2008-09-09T12:24:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73e1d0b1-d01b-4800-96f8-ce3bb2db575f@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<clu6b4lndhsm8i8bdald9fsgjh238a8v9m@4ax.com> <e41e894f-f8aa-4e3f-9b6b-932190cacd0d@8g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <6hm5g2FmvtrfU1@mid.individual.net> <0001HW.C4DB87AB00094E66B01AD9AF@news.suddenlink.net> <6hnrnpFn895uU1@mid.individual.net> <i1idb4taqqtg1hljse3s29066um2p6l25i@4ax.com> <LoDtk.589215$I42.585149@fe04.news.easynews.com>`

```
On Aug 28, 3:55 pm, "William M. Klein" <wmkl...@nospam.netcom.com>
wrote:
> (getting back - I think - to the original topic),
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> meet my requirements - that I used to be able to meet - are declining".

I read this post several days ago, but wanted to mull it over for a
while before Replying. I think the point that Mr. Klein has made here
is salient, even profound, well, to me anyway, as it does resonate
with my experiences.

Like this: As the IT industry goes through its technological changes,
its market forces shifts, waxing and waning of demand for and
availability of resources (skilled people, language and tool support,
OS and hardware support), and the "disruptions" that result from all
this, it is worthwhile to ask: What is it that _I_ want to _preserve_?

If what one wants to preserve, even _insisting_ to preserve, is the
"top billing rate", then your perception of decline will be much
steeper than if one has a more, uh, flexible viewpoint.

Sure, _all other things being equal_, a higher bill rate is better
than a lower one, and a higher salary is better than a lower one.

My own inclination has been to be moved more by "it is generally the
case that all other things are _not_ equal".

So if you love doing CoBOL just because it is CoBOL, whatever the
reason, twisted or not, you can probably (even most certainly) still
do it. But then, to preserve this, you must let other things Die. The
belief or illusion that you can still maintain top dollar. The
insistence that you live within 20 miles of the client/customer/
employer. The desire that you always work in an environment that will
permit you to stay with cutting edge technology. Or your preference to
always be an independent contractor rather than a full-time employee.

No, these are things that, that time to time, you may have to give up.

There's nothing wrong with that. People make these kinds of choices
all the time. It's OK to give up CoBOL to preserve your bill rate. It
is also OK to give up some of your bill rate in order to live closer
to your family. And it's certainly OK to change your mind about all of
these things. Otherwise, it would be a very dull world.

>
> P.S.  It often surprises me how this forum is dominated by "contractors" rather
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Who besides Robert, and DocDwarf, and I are contractors? :-)

Also, I "appreciate" :-) Bill's reference to "employed CoBOL
programmers" apparently being restricted to "full time employees".
Reminds me of all the times my Significant Other implored me to give
up "contracting" and get a "real job" :-).

Once, she said to me, "Ugh! Where I work we think that only people who
can't get real, full-time employment jobs become contractors."

My response: "Really? My contracting companies tell me that the only
reason we can get contracting jobs is because their full-time
employees can't get the work done."

So, take your pick, and appreciate the differences.

Ken

> --
> Bill Klein
>  wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Declining Cobol job market

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "klshafer@att.net" <klshafer@att.net>
- **Date:** 2008-09-11T07:02:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d4118d60-6c34-4bd6-a8e4-d6795182560b@26g2000hsk.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<clu6b4lndhsm8i8bdald9fsgjh238a8v9m@4ax.com> <e41e894f-f8aa-4e3f-9b6b-932190cacd0d@8g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <6hm5g2FmvtrfU1@mid.individual.net> <0001HW.C4DB87AB00094E66B01AD9AF@news.suddenlink.net> <6hnrnpFn895uU1@mid.individual.net> <i1idb4taqqtg1hljse3s29066um2p6l25i@4ax.com> <LoDtk.589215$I42.585149@fe04.news.easynews.com> <73e1d0b1-d01b-4800-96f8-ce3bb2db575f@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <dfodc49h0ne34k0i5j7ag6mrrrlih9juff@4ax.com>`

```
On Sep 9, 4:52 pm, Howard Brazee <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
> On Tue, 9 Sep 2008 12:24:08 -0700 (PDT), "klsha...@att.net"
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes, Mr. Brazee, this is a well-considered "trade-off". Pension and
health care benefits are, in my opinion, the two attributes of full-
time employment that must be weighed very carefully in making a
decision regarding contracting.

On the other hand, long-term trends are leveling even these
distinctions. How so? Employing companies are paying less and less of
the health care premiums, and also are moving toward Health Care
Savings (HSA) accounts, where the employee is obligated to put up a
pool of money for the little stuff and the insurance covers the bug
stuff (say, above a $2500 deductible). Similarly with pensions, the
trend is away from "defined benefit" plans to "defined contribution"
plans, like 401K's. These trends are all designed to shift somewhat
more risk onto the employee. All of this means evaluating the risk/
reward factors can get pretty complicated.

ken
```

```
<klshafer@att.net> wrote:

>On the other hand, long-term trends are leveling even these
>distinctions. How so? Employing companies are paying less and less of
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>reward factors can get pretty complicated.

In my case, working for the University of Colorado, my pension is
PERA, and I also have some "defined contribution" plans.  I did buy
some time as well, but will have a much lower pension than my peers
who will be retiring with 30 years.  

I also noted that there was a trend for fewer contracts that did not
require moving or travel.   My children's families are local, and I am
addicted to sleeping with my wife.   Those are valuable
considerations.    Moving also interferes with financial strategies of
home-ownership, especially if the contract wants you to move when your
old house is in the wrong side of a selling cycle.
```

```
>Reminds me of all the times my Significant Other implored me to give
>up "contracting" and get a "real job" :-).
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>So, take your pick, and appreciate the differences.

'Anyone who thinks all fruits ripen at the same time as strawberries knows 
nothing of grapes.' - Paracelsus

'It takes all kinds to make a world... sure glad *I* ain't one of 'em!' 
(attributed to various sources)

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Declining Cobol job market

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-09-09T21:04:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kj0ec4t326r0arvsfk9g931uk09gjktiqg@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<clu6b4lndhsm8i8bdald9fsgjh238a8v9m@4ax.com> <e41e894f-f8aa-4e3f-9b6b-932190cacd0d@8g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <6hm5g2FmvtrfU1@mid.individual.net> <0001HW.C4DB87AB00094E66B01AD9AF@news.suddenlink.net> <6hnrnpFn895uU1@mid.individual.net> <i1idb4taqqtg1hljse3s29066um2p6l25i@4ax.com> <LoDtk.589215$I42.585149@fe04.news.easynews.com> <73e1d0b1-d01b-4800-96f8-ce3bb2db575f@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Tue, 9 Sep 2008 12:24:08 -0700 (PDT), "klshafer@att.net" <klshafer@att.net> wrote:

>On Aug 28, 3:55ï¿½pm, "William M. Klein" <wmkl...@nospam.netcom.com>
>wrote:
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>steeper than if one has a more, uh, flexible viewpoint.

Contractors used to make significantly more than perm employees. Now, they make about the
same or less on an annual basis. The earnings killer is unemployment between gigs, which
reduces their annual income 10-40%. Add the cost of moving every 6-12 months (1000),
higher priced housing on short-term leases (+300), furniture rental (300) and private
health insurance (600).  

To be specific, perm Cobol pays $35-40/hr + benefits, contracting pays $45-50/hr. YMMV.

>Sure, _all other things being equal_, a higher bill rate is better
>than a lower one, and a higher salary is better than a lower one.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>belief or illusion that you can still maintain top dollar. 

Cobol has not been top dollar in the last ten years, when I started contracting. The rate
is flat or even falling. Java and .NET pay $15-20/hr more than Cobol.

>The insistence that you live within 20 miles of the client/customer/
>employer.

If you have a house and family, DO NOT try to be a contractor. Your first gig will be near
home; your second and subsequent will be hundreds of miles away. You'll try to make it
home every weekend,  give up after a month. I've seen it happen to others a dozen times.
The only exception might be Washington DC, which has high contractor density. 

I go home for lunch. I try to live within walking distance, always within 7 miles. In
Pittsburgh, I lived at the top of the Monongahela Incline, a cable car & major tourist
attraction, worked at the bottom (Station Square). I had a million dollar postcard view of
the city for $600/mo. Every gig I've done was a nice place to live, including downtown St
Louis when it had the highest crime rate in the US (statistics don't tell the whole
story). I have enough sense to avoid Brooklyn and downtown Detroit. 

>The desire that you always work in an environment that will
>permit you to stay with cutting edge technology. Or your preference to
>always be an independent contractor rather than a full-time employee.

Or a pathalogical environment. Sometimes you know the first day; other times they're able
to hide it until you've been there a month and committed to an apartment lease. A red flag
should go up if they hire contractors for day-to-day maintenance that should be done by
employees. There is probably a hidden agenda. Good signs are a defined project and an
outsource contract (unless it's with IBM).

>There's nothing wrong with that. People make these kinds of choices
>all the time. It's OK to give up CoBOL to preserve your bill rate.

As a former futures and options trader, I'm accustomed to thinking price is a function of
supply and demand. In this case, we have an illiquid market -- there are no buyers of
non-mainframe Cobol at ANY price. The few buyers want niche skills such as Lawson or
Siebel, or have a hidden agenda, like the place in Pittsburgh.

>Also, I "appreciate" :-) Bill's reference to "employed CoBOL
>programmers" apparently being restricted to "full time employees".
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>employees can't get the work done."

Or the manager is an ogre. Or the company wants sacrificial goats for the next round of
cuts. Or there are kickbacks going on.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Declining Cobol job market

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-09-11T00:14:56+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6ips21Fr0sf2U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<clu6b4lndhsm8i8bdald9fsgjh238a8v9m@4ax.com> <e41e894f-f8aa-4e3f-9b6b-932190cacd0d@8g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <6hm5g2FmvtrfU1@mid.individual.net> <0001HW.C4DB87AB00094E66B01AD9AF@news.suddenlink.net> <6hnrnpFn895uU1@mid.individual.net> <i1idb4taqqtg1hljse3s29066um2p6l25i@4ax.com> <LoDtk.589215$I42.585149@fe04.news.easynews.com> <73e1d0b1-d01b-4800-96f8-ce3bb2db575f@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <kj0ec4t326r0arvsfk9g931uk09gjktiqg@4ax.com>`

```


"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:kj0ec4t326r0arvsfk9g931uk09gjktiqg@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 9 Sep 2008 12:24:08 -0700 (PDT), "klshafer@att.net" 
> <klshafer@att.net> wrote:
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
> reduces their annual income 10-40%.

Hmmm... interested to hear you say that. I started contracting in 1975 in 
Europe. Since then the longest I have been out of work when I didn't want to 
be, was 10 days. Usually, I can be working within 7 days of completing a 
contract and a lot of the time it is contiguous. However, I do often take 6 
month (or longer) breaks between contracts. I didn't in the early days 
because I couldn't afford to. As I made more cash I took longer breaks. 
During the last 15 years or so I have taken to having Summers (say 4 months) 
home in NZ. Next year, I'll return to Europe for serious work and won't take 
a holiday for probably 2 years, after which I plan to come home permanently 
and retire. This is a lifestyle I could never have achieved by working as a 
full time employee, even though there are people who can.

I agree that enforced unemployment is a very bad thing if you're a 
contractor, but given a marketplace with over 300 million people in it, is 
there any real reason to be unemployed?

I would say: "Find out what they want and need and are paying well for. Be 
that."

It may mean broadening skill sets, and there are many for whom that doesn't 
seem to be an option. I don't understand it, but I accept that that is the 
case. For myself, if someone was paying, say, a couple of thousand a day to 
translate Mandarin Chinese into Swahili, I'd be fluent in both languages 
within 18 months... :-) (The fact that I am reasonably fluent in more than 
one language is not because I love languages; it simply gives me more 
countries to market my skills in... I put in the time and paid the dues and 
received very adequate recompense. The spin off was living and working in 
different cultures and assimilating into them, becoming part of the 
community. That is good for personal growth.)

(We have a saying here in NZ, were speech tends to be direct and to the 
point: "If a man will pay me a dollar for every shovelful of shit I can 
shovel, I'll shovel shit for a living.")

We also believe that the world doesn't owe us a living and it is necessary 
to make some effort.

(However, with the growing affluence of the country and the increasing 
availability of benefits for all kinds of things, I can see this Victorian 
work ethic falling into decline... :-). We have kids now who have never 
worked a day in their lives and have no intention of ever doing so. (If you 
saw the beaches here, you'd understand it better...:-)) I don't judge them 
and I certainly don't think they are "wrong"... an affluent nation with a 
small population SHOULD take care of its citizens, but I do think they are 
missing out on much. )




>Add the cost of moving every 6-12 months (1000),
> higher priced housing on short-term leases (+300), furniture rental (300) 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> $45-50/hr. YMMV.

Then it is definitely time to move out of COBOL contracting, as I believe 
you are doing. n this context, I can see how a permanent job has some 
attraction. Regular income, paid holidays, etc.

>
>>Sure, _all other things being equal_, a higher bill rate is better
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>>belief or illusion that you can still maintain top dollar.

If you love COBOL, you can write it all your life. (I probably will, 
although some problems I have been dealing with recently have more to do 
with COBOL than with other languages...) You just won't make much of a 
living out of it as long as you write it for someone else.

>
> Cobol has not been top dollar in the last ten years, when I started 
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> story). I have enough sense to avoid Brooklyn and downtown Detroit.

Yep, me too. I usually get the contract then move to a house or apartment 
close to the site. The advice you give above gels with my experience, except 
that I do have a house. Certainly, the mobility required for successful 
contracting, kind of precludes doing it if you have a family.

(I don't let the house while I'm away, partly because I love watching Estate 
Agents squirming when they are unable to persuade me to do so :-), but 
mainly because it is my home and not an investment property. I retain my 
housekeeper and gardener who have been with me for years, to keep an eye on 
it when I'm away. Fortunately, I have excellent neighbours and that helps 
too. I can go away for a couple of years, come home and the beds have fresh 
linen on them, there is fresh milk, bread, and butter in the refrigerator, 
my car has been made road legal and starts immediately, and everything is 
cleaned and aired. I worked very hard for many years to achieve this, and 
now I enjoy it immensely... :-))


>
>>The desire that you always work in an environment that will
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> Siebel, or have a hidden agenda, like the place in Pittsburgh.

I understand that's a high risk occupation Robert, but wouldn't you be 
better off doing that? If I had those skills, I'd be doing that.

Pete.
```

```
> On Tue, 09 Sep 2008 21:04:01 -0500, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> >Every gig I've done was a nice place to live, including downtown St
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, some places are more difficult to like than others - they take
more effort. And yet, just as you have implied, every place has
something unique to offer. It is the right thing to do to be receptive
to those blessings.

I have some wistfulness that with the decline of CoBOL in
manufacturing because of the ascendence of ERP systems, it is more
difficult to find engagements in manufacturing cities, which owing to
historical transportation needs, tended to be "river cities".

ken
```

- **References:** `<6hm5g2FmvtrfU1@mid.individual.net> <0001HW.C4DB87AB00094E66B01AD9AF@news.suddenlink.net> <6hnrnpFn895uU1@mid.individual.net> <i1idb4taqqtg1hljse3s29066um2p6l25i@4ax.com> <LoDtk.589215$I42.585149@fe04.news.easynews.com> <73e1d0b1-d01b-4800-96f8-ce3bb2db575f@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <kj0ec4t326r0arvsfk9g931uk09gjktiqg@4ax.com> <g4kfc4dvq9874ek72udeddfm89ho9h24f3@4ax.com> <045909d1-f3c9-42ea-a138-ce10010a8f9e@a1g2000hsb.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Thu, 11 Sep 2008 07:37:13 -0700 (PDT), "klshafer@att.net"
<klshafer@att.net> wrote:

>I have some wistfulness that with the decline of CoBOL in
>manufacturing because of the ascendence of ERP systems, it is more
>difficult to find engagements in manufacturing cities, which owing to
>historical transportation needs, tended to be "river cities".

I've read that the largest city not designed around water
transportation is Atlanta - named after the Atlantic Coast Line, and
built at a projected spot in that line.   Dallas is larger, designed
to be as far up the river as was navigatable - but was actually
farther up than they thought.   

I wouldn't be surprised if there were some new Chinese city without
water transportation which has surpassed them.

These days, it isn't obvious that Denver used the Platt river to move
beaver pelts to market - old transportation needs have changed.   Of
course, the need to have water supplied to cities hasn't.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Declining Cobol job market

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "klshafer@att.net" <klshafer@att.net>
- **Date:** 2008-09-11T07:16:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d770ca15-dec2-4111-a751-0dd173293f6e@w7g2000hsa.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<clu6b4lndhsm8i8bdald9fsgjh238a8v9m@4ax.com> <e41e894f-f8aa-4e3f-9b6b-932190cacd0d@8g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <6hm5g2FmvtrfU1@mid.individual.net> <0001HW.C4DB87AB00094E66B01AD9AF@news.suddenlink.net> <6hnrnpFn895uU1@mid.individual.net> <i1idb4taqqtg1hljse3s29066um2p6l25i@4ax.com> <LoDtk.589215$I42.585149@fe04.news.easynews.com> <73e1d0b1-d01b-4800-96f8-ce3bb2db575f@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <kj0ec4t326r0arvsfk9g931uk09gjktiqg@4ax.com>`

```
On Sep 9, 10:04 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> On Tue, 9 Sep 2008 12:24:08 -0700 (PDT), "klsha...@att.net" <klsha...@att.net> wrote:
> >On Aug 28, 3:55 pm, "William M. Klein" <wmkl...@nospam.netcom.com>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> health insurance (600).  

Good summary of the "incidental costs" associated with contracting. My
experience agrees with yours on the increased risk of downtime, since
2000-2001. Prior to 2000-2001 I seldom needed more than a month
between projects. Post 2000-2001 it has increased significantly. My
response: look for projects with a chance of multi-year duration if I
can find them. Even if it means a significantly lower rate.

>
> To be specific, perm Cobol pays $35-40/hr + benefits, contracting pays $45-50/hr. YMMV.

We should allow for some of the geographic differences alluded to
earlier. Generally, the midwest is $5-$10/hour less than either coast,
and I would hazard that Chicago is closer rate-wise to the coasts than
"the rest of the midwest."

>
> >Sure, _all other things being equal_, a higher bill rate is better
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> is flat or even falling. Java and .NET pay $15-20/hr more than Cobol.

Agreed. I have even seen this "formalized" in procurement "preferred
vendor" rate quotes by "category." Maybe the spread is only $10-$15/
hour, but it is still quite significant.

>
> >The insistence that you live within 20 miles of the client/customer/
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> The only exception might be Washington DC, which has high contractor density.

Yes, it is difficult to still be a contractor with a house and family,
but not impossible. This was not so 10-15 years ago. Nowadays, I would
not advise it except to the young and single, and the "older" :-) who
are empty-nesters (children now grown) and looking for a little
"adventure". And what _can_ work very well is if one spouse has a
"conventional" job with good benefits (health care and pension) and
the other takes the "risks" (hopefully, a little more money, but no
health-care and pension is needed because that is covered by the
spouse.)

>
> I go home for lunch. I try to live within walking distance, always within 7 miles. In
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> story). I have enough sense to avoid Brooklyn and downtown Detroit.

This is excellent, isn't it! You are being paid to have an
(occasional) "vacation" all over America! I didn't have as much
"sense" as you, though, some of my "vacation" was taken in downtown
Detroit and Cleveland :-).

>
> >The desire that you always work in an environment that will
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> outsource contract (unless it's with IBM).

Hmmmm... I wouldn't call it a "red flag", but maybe a "yellow flag."
Long-term day-to-day maintenance gigs can also be a good thing (shall
we ask the 'Doc?) They come with their own specific context, that's
for sure, but no more than the development/enhancement kinds of
projects. Usually we can figure it out.

>
> >There's nothing wrong with that. People make these kinds of choices
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> cuts. Or there are kickbacks going on.- Hide quoted text -

Different agendas, whether overt or hidden, as you say, can make for
very different kinds of "success" on projects. Determining what this
is is part of the job of a contractor. You get better at it over time.

Thanks for the good response, Robert...

ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Declining Cobol job market

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()

- **Message-ID:** `<gabpbf$k26$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<clu6b4lndhsm8i8bdald9fsgjh238a8v9m@4ax.com> <73e1d0b1-d01b-4800-96f8-ce3bb2db575f@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <kj0ec4t326r0arvsfk9g931uk09gjktiqg@4ax.com> <d770ca15-dec2-4111-a751-0dd173293f6e@w7g2000hsa.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <d770ca15-dec2-4111-a751-0dd173293f6e@w7g2000hsa.googlegroups.com>,
klshafer@att.net <klshafer@att.net> wrote:

[snip]

>Hmmmm... I wouldn't call it a "red flag", but maybe a "yellow flag."
>Long-term day-to-day maintenance gigs can also be a good thing (shall
>we ask the 'Doc?)

zzzZZZZzzzzzz... zzzaaaAAWWWKKKHHHHHhhhh... eh? whuh?  Oh, I'm sorry, I 
was just... mapping out some memory locations in the acoustic tiles, 
that's all... did I miss something?

Things here are a bit... unsteady.  Today they announced the fourth new 
Corner Officerino di tutti Corner Officerini in nine-months-and-change; 
folks are *mighty* annoyed that all the sucking-up they did to the 
previous fellow is for naught.

Last week I probably came close to getting myself fired; the core of it 
was telling my Tech Lead something along the lines of 'Mr Baker, I have no 
idea where you learned to talk to people like that.  I have no idea whom 
you speak to in that manner who responds in a positive fashion... but I 
will tell you that if you ever again tell me to 'shut up and do the work' 
you're going to be talking to an empty chair.  I do not address people in 
the workplace in that manner; I will not tolerate being addressed in the 
workplace in that manner.  Have I made myself clear?'

Things got straightened out, more or less... and besides, there's New 
Management to deal with.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Declining Cobol job market

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2008-09-11T13:42:26-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0ssic4hin686v72k22eflrqs9vvsv79mu3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<clu6b4lndhsm8i8bdald9fsgjh238a8v9m@4ax.com> <73e1d0b1-d01b-4800-96f8-ce3bb2db575f@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <kj0ec4t326r0arvsfk9g931uk09gjktiqg@4ax.com> <d770ca15-dec2-4111-a751-0dd173293f6e@w7g2000hsa.googlegroups.com> <gabpbf$k26$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Thu, 11 Sep 2008 18:51:27 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>Last week I probably came close to getting myself fired; the core of it 
>was telling my Tech Lead something along the lines of 'Mr Baker, I have no 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>workplace in that manner.  Have I made myself clear?'

I was talking with a sports fan the other day, and he was noting a
basketball coach who got very emotional, swearing and such with one
player - but never with another player.   My friend said the coach had
learned which style works with which player.    In some environments
(such as the NBA), the coaches are more likely to be fired than the
players.     

One trouble is - many managers only learn one style.   Hopefully your
manager is smart enough to adjust.

Another trouble is figuring out the best way to have multiple styles
in a small organization.    You might find it about as uncomfortable
watching someone else being berated as yourself - even if that's what
works on that someone else.
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:0ssic4hin686v72k22eflrqs9vvsv79mu3@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 11 Sep 2008 18:51:27 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Very profound.  Certainly, different strokes for different folks, but there 
are behaviours that are just unacceptable. I won't tolerate rudeness, 
prejudice, or any kind of bullying (except from me, of course... :-)) on my 
projects. I've had several occasions where it was necessary to nip this in 
the bud.

The way I put it was: "Each of you has a perfect right to your personal 
opinions and how you feel about things. But you leave it at the door when 
you come to work. Colleagues will be treated with respect and 
professionalism. You don't have to like each other (though things go much 
better if you do) but you do have to work together. If anyone objects to 
this or feels very strongly about some particular aspect of it, speak up 
now, or see me later." No-one ever came to see me and I don't recall anyone 
speaking up either... :-) I would seriously consider transferring anyone who 
could not comply with such a policy, off my project.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Declining Cobol job market

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-09-12T15:37:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gae2br$2tn$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<clu6b4lndhsm8i8bdald9fsgjh238a8v9m@4ax.com> <d770ca15-dec2-4111-a751-0dd173293f6e@w7g2000hsa.googlegroups.com> <gabpbf$k26$1@reader1.panix.com> <6ivet2FoutvU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <6ivet2FoutvU1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:gabpbf$k26$1@reader1.panix.com...

[snip]

>> I have no idea whom
>> you speak to in that manner who responds in a positive fashion... but I
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>it.

Perhaps so... perhaps no.  As behaviorial psychologists have noted, people 
tend to replicate the behaviors of those in their community who are seen 
as respected and repeat the behaviors that bring them what they call 
'rewards'.  If an Upper Manager is seen, in a given organisation, as a 
person who tells folks 'do what I tell you, without question, or get 
fired' then a Lower Manager would be expected to behave similarly... 
reinforcement of 'we-group' principles and all that.

I've worked in what I've called 'sick shops' for a few years, Mr Dashwood, 
and have seen things like... oh, say, a consultant/contractor/hired gun 
who has been doing what was called 'Good Work' for two, three years... 
until (behind closed doors) he disagreed with an approach his manager was 
taking on a certain project.  He was escorted out by Security and the 
following week I heard this interchange between the manager and his 
director:

D: 'What happened to Jones... wasn't he working with you on the REAPS 
project?'

M: 'Oh, I had to let him go... he just didn't fit in, you know, he wasn't 
fitting in with the Way We Do Things Here.'

D: 'I've seen that before... well, let's get someone else in.'

Now... to me it would make perfect sense to close the door, sit the 
manager down and say 'Jones was cream-of-the-crop for two, three years and 
then, all of a sudden, 'just didn't fit in'?  What, exactly, is going on 
here?'... but I also have never been a Director so what do I know?

As the adage has it, 'A fish rots from the head'.  As was attributed to 
Jay Gould, a railroad tycoon ('robber baron') in the USA, 'I can hire half 
of the working class to kill the other half'.

>>
>> Things got straightened out, more or less... and besides, there's New
>> Management to deal with.
>>
>Let's hope they are better... :-)

As noted above... they most likely did not get into the position of being 
Next-In-Line-To-The-Top by being *so* very different from what was, 
previously, The Top.

Me?  I'se jes' a COBOL-codin' fool.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Declining Cobol job market

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-09-11T20:36:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dsbjc4hno03kh0s7v1l9c7srvibs2fssk6@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<e41e894f-f8aa-4e3f-9b6b-932190cacd0d@8g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <6hm5g2FmvtrfU1@mid.individual.net> <0001HW.C4DB87AB00094E66B01AD9AF@news.suddenlink.net> <6hnrnpFn895uU1@mid.individual.net> <i1idb4taqqtg1hljse3s29066um2p6l25i@4ax.com> <LoDtk.589215$I42.585149@fe04.news.easynews.com> <73e1d0b1-d01b-4800-96f8-ce3bb2db575f@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <kj0ec4t326r0arvsfk9g931uk09gjktiqg@4ax.com> <d770ca15-dec2-4111-a751-0dd173293f6e@w7g2000hsa.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Thu, 11 Sep 2008 07:16:55 -0700 (PDT), "klshafer@att.net" <klshafer@att.net> wrote:

>On Sep 9, 10:04ï¿½pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
>> On Tue, 9 Sep 2008 12:24:08 -0700 (PDT), "klsha...@att.net" <klsha...@att.net> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>can find them. Even if it means a significantly lower rate.

Position of multi-year duration at a lower rate sounds like the definition of a perm job.
:)

>> To be specific, perm Cobol pays $35-40/hr + benefits, contracting pays $45-50/hr. YMMV.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>"the rest of the midwest."

I received 'coastal rates' in St Louis, Minneapolis and Champaign, and all over the South.
Low rates are found in places with a tradition of low pay. Three that come to mind are
Chattanooga, anywhere in Iowa and Santa Fe NM. Ohio tends to be low, and culturally
bankrupt (a recent development, northern Ohio used to be soulful).

It's best to avoid small towns because there's nothing to do, and they have mediocre
restaurants and supermarkets. Bentonville AR comes to mind. Bentonville-Rogers has a
population of 70K and surprisingly expensive housing (I paid $1,800/mo), but feels like a
small town. My favorite restaurant there was IHOP. 

>> >Sure, _all other things being equal_, a higher bill rate is better
>> >than a lower one, and a higher salary is better than a lower one.
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>spouse.)

I prefer a mobile spouse who moves with me, which rules out a career type job for her.
Many/most Indian contractors have a spouse who is also a contractor. Inevitably, they work
in different cities, see each other every three months. I refuse to live like that.

If you're playing the per diem game, and you're foolish not to, Uncle Sam requires you to
move to another city every year. If you work in a city more than 365 days, you've lost
your tax home, you've moved. High cost of living places are BETTER, because your tax free
income (per diem) is so much higher (>$200/day) and apartments cost the same as everywhere
else -- $800/mo. Exceptions are NYC and San Francisco, where city housing really IS
expensive. Affordable housing is available, but you must commute for an hour, which I
refuse to do.

>> I go home for lunch. I try to live within walking distance, always within 7 miles. In
>> Pittsburgh, I lived at the top of the Monongahela Incline, a cable car & major tourist
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>(occasional) "vacation" all over America.

Yep. I prefer executive apartments in the cultural hub of the city, and weekend trips to
area attractions. In Albany NY, I lived on Lark Street, the Greenwich Village of upstate
NY, a block from the Capitol, went every weekend to the Berkshires, Lake George, Vermont,
once to Cape Cod. My wife said we didn't need a TV, we could just look out the living room
window, there was free street theater 24/7. In St Louis we lived in Soulard, an utterly
charming neighborhood of 1890s brick buildings conducive to evening walks. In such places,
you learn to think like a local rather than a tourist.

The best places I've lived are
Urban - Houston Galleria district
Suburban - Morristown NJ
                  Chicago, N and NW suburbs
Surrounding countryside - Jacksonville FL

I dislike old cities where the city center was abandoned by white flight, you must drive
to the suburbs for shopping, restaurants, even a supermarket. Cities like that were
Albany, Pittsburgh, St Louis and Jacksonville. 

>> >The desire that you always work in an environment that will
>> >permit you to stay with cutting edge technology. Or your preference to
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>projects. Usually we can figure it out.

Management sees acquisitions as revenue producers, maintenance as a revenue sink. When
they have to make cuts or outsource, maintainers get the axe. 

>> >There's nothing wrong with that. People make these kinds of choices
>> >all the time. It's OK to give up CoBOL to preserve your bill rate.
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>is is part of the job of a contractor. You get better at it over time.

You're a pawn on a chessboard you do not control and sometimes cannot see, at least in the
beginning.
```

```
On Tue, 09 Sep 2008 21:04:01 -0500, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:

>On Tue, 9 Sep 2008 12:24:08 -0700 (PDT), "klshafer@att.net" <klshafer@att.net> wrote:
>
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
>To be specific, perm Cobol pays $35-40/hr + benefits, contracting pays $45-50/hr. YMMV.
Again your are generalizing something that is not true. When making
statements like this you need first to base your theories, then, as on
this particular case you are speaking about salaries, you need to
specify very clearly in which part of the world those values apply.

I can say that where I work, many COBOL/Mainframe contractors are
making twice what permanent stuff is doing after taxes in both sides.
Most of these contractors do not need to move, so the costs you are
speaking of dont even apply, and again most of them stay with the same
client for a few years (myself has been 5 years, then 2 years, now 3
years in straight contracts).
As a benefit over permanent stuff, I have 7-8 weeks holidays per year,
flexi time, and payment is as mentioned above even taking in
consideration that I work less hours per year than they do.


Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

```
On Thu, 18 Sep 2008 00:30:26 +0100, Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
wrote:

>On Tue, 09 Sep 2008 21:04:01 -0500, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:
>
…[50 more quoted lines elided]…
>specify very clearly in which part of the world those values apply.

USA

>I can say that where I work, many COBOL/Mainframe contractors are
>making twice what permanent stuff is doing after taxes in both sides.

That WAS true here before Y2K. 

>Most of these contractors do not need to move, so the costs you are
>speaking of dont even apply, and again most of them stay with the same
>client for a few years (myself has been 5 years, then 2 years, now 3
>years in straight contracts).
>As a benefit over permanent stuff, I have 7-8 weeks holidays per year,

US contractors get no paid holidays (vacations), nor sick days, nor national holidays. If
we don't work, we don't get paid. 

>flexi time, and payment is as mentioned above even taking in
>consideration that I work less hours per year than they do.

We DO get paid for overtime, but are seldom asked to work long hours. Extra money isn't in
the budget.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Declining Cobol job market

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-09-10T23:24:03+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6ipp2lFs0q2sU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<clu6b4lndhsm8i8bdald9fsgjh238a8v9m@4ax.com> <e41e894f-f8aa-4e3f-9b6b-932190cacd0d@8g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <6hm5g2FmvtrfU1@mid.individual.net> <0001HW.C4DB87AB00094E66B01AD9AF@news.suddenlink.net> <6hnrnpFn895uU1@mid.individual.net> <i1idb4taqqtg1hljse3s29066um2p6l25i@4ax.com> <LoDtk.589215$I42.585149@fe04.news.easynews.com> <73e1d0b1-d01b-4800-96f8-ce3bb2db575f@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`

```


<klshafer@att.net> wrote in message 
news:73e1d0b1-d01b-4800-96f8-ce3bb2db575f@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com...
On Aug 28, 3:55 pm, "William M. Klein" <wmkl...@nospam.netcom.com>
wrote:

>Who besides Robert, and DocDwarf, and I are contractors? :-)

Me. But only for the last 30 years, so it may have escaped your notice :-)

Before I was managing projects on contract and providing advice to 
corporations on contract, I was programming computers on contract (mostly in 
COBOL, because that's where the money was, and because I really liked COBOL 
best, of the languages I knew...)

I think you made some good points about different strokes for different 
folks, and I completely agree that there is a different mindset required for 
contracting or permanent.

(And I don't think one is "superior"to the other, although I would never 
work full time for a corporation because it doesn't suit the criteria that I 
personally require to do the job.)

Of course, "permanent" isn't, and hasn't been for decades. I have seen 
people who gave their lives to corporations fired without compunction when 
it suited the company. Business is business. Anyone who thinks they have a 
"job for life" either has a very short life expectancy or is deluding 
themselves.

Cobol as a technology is declining mainly because it doesn't well fit 
today's networking requirements (although there are many other reasons as 
well.) As the language declines, so will the job opportunities; it is 
inevitable, and has nothing to do with "contract" or "permanent" status, or 
people's perceptions of what they used to get and can't any more.

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<clu6b4lndhsm8i8bdald9fsgjh238a8v9m@4ax.com> <e41e894f-f8aa-4e3f-9b6b-932190cacd0d@8g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <6hm5g2FmvtrfU1@mid.individual.net> <0001HW.C4DB87AB00094E66B01AD9AF@news.suddenlink.net> <6hnrnpFn895uU1@mid.individual.net> <i1idb4taqqtg1hljse3s29066um2p6l25i@4ax.com> <LoDtk.589215$I42.585149@fe04.news.easynews.com> <73e1d0b1-d01b-4800-96f8-ce3bb2db575f@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <6ipp2lFs0q2sU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Sep 10, 7:24 am, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> <klsha...@att.net> wrote in message
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Me. But only for the last 30 years, so it may have escaped your notice :-)

Welcome back, Mr. Pete! No it didn't escape my notice. I just was
under the impression that you left the confines of Contracting for the
greener pastures of True Consulting some time ago. But hey, if you're
tired of the taste of champagne and you deign to rub shoulders with us
beer drinkers (contractors) again, are we not obliged to welcome you
back with a slap on your shoulder and a hearty Har Har Har HAR HAR!
We'll let you buy the next round, though :-)...

>
> Before I was managing projects on contract and providing advice to
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> contracting or permanent.

Or maybe could be bio-physical. I remember a nutritionist telling me
after an examination several years ago that I had run down adrenals -
she put me on some adrenal extract supplements. I still remember her
comment, "With all the running around and traveling that you do with
your contracting job, I'm not surprised." There may be a bit of
"contractor high" (similar to the proverbial "runner's high"?) that
goes with the territory.

>
> (And I don't think one is "superior"to the other, although I would never
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

As I have alluded to before, though a single individual can not
"change the market", he can ACTIVELY change the way he "engages" the
market. Learning new skills is certainly a very good way to adjust, as
you have amply demonstrated. But so is changing your attitude and your
perceptions, as I set forth in the list of "things you may have to
consider giving up" above.

How can I put this most succinctly?  Like this...

All around me, Delphi and Visteon workers are being asked to take cuts
which would bring them down to $14-$18/hour from $25-$30/hour.

Q: Given that, if I am asked to take a cut 30% off the Y2K peak rate,
what is that to me?

A: Very little.

Once you detach your notion of self-worth from the rate that you get,
all this becomes a lot easier. And it allows one to be what one truly
wants to be.

ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Declining Cobol job market

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-09-13T02:59:54+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6ivefcFol6rU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<6hm5g2FmvtrfU1@mid.individual.net> <0001HW.C4DB87AB00094E66B01AD9AF@news.suddenlink.net> <6hnrnpFn895uU1@mid.individual.net> <i1idb4taqqtg1hljse3s29066um2p6l25i@4ax.com> <LoDtk.589215$I42.585149@fe04.news.easynews.com> <73e1d0b1-d01b-4800-96f8-ce3bb2db575f@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <6ipp2lFs0q2sU1@mid.individual.net> <68ca64de-8939-47e4-bca2-efd13906de91@a70g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <S4ayk.22778$89.1973@nlpi069.nbdc.sbc.com> <bhfic4hrc7v5dmv6a9fpu4g3uiddfjk950@4ax.com>`

```


"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:bhfic4hrc7v5dmv6a9fpu4g3uiddfjk950@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 11 Sep 2008 09:39:42 -0500, "Michael Mattias"
> <mmattias@talsystems.com> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
:-)

I have been a contracted consultant for around 10 years now.

I "build houses" and I assist managers to avoid blame.

I believe consultancy is about providing advice. If I give them advice, and 
they take it, then any negative outcome is really not their fault.

This awesome responsibility which, despite my flippancy here, I actually 
take very seriously, has proven to be quite rewarding, both financially and 
in terms of personal satisfaction.

Shaping policy is a lot different from dancing round it.

Wouldn't you rather work in a place where policy decisions are carefully 
considered, advice is sought and alternatives are evaluated, where there is 
an ongoing strategy that is coherent and goals are clear, than one where 
they just make it up as they go along? or simply knee jerk when their 
opposition does something?

Sure, there are times when what has to be done is not popular, but my 
experience has been that complete openness with the people who are going to 
have to implement the policy goes a long way. I have never had a problem 
with explaining why a certain course has been adopted and why the 
alternatives were not considered acceptable. And if it transpires that there 
were key things that were missed (it doesn't very often but I can think of 
one particular case in point) then I have no problem with doing everything I 
can to get the policy corrected.

A lot of high level decision making should really be about what's right and 
not who's right. Unfortunately, this is seldom the case, particularly in 
older, more conservative companies where they still cling to rigidly 
hierarchic management structures. When you've spent most of your life 
clawing your way up the corporate ladder, it is not easy to delegate the 
power you now have to someone else. Managers have egos and they don't want 
to lose face. Employing consultants is a good way to not have to.

The main problem that most people have with consultants is that they are 
highly regarded by management (this has more to do with their cost, than any 
particular intrinisic value...see below), but do not necessarily understand 
the company and its problems, advise short term strategies which they never 
have to implement, and really don't give a rat's arse about the workforce.

(You can see I've worked with Arthur Andersen, or, more correctly, had to 
clean up the mess... :-))

However, not all consultants are bad. Just as in everything else, there are 
good ones and bad ones.

I was once contracted to provide a "health check" on a major corporation's 
IT systems. I did the job and provided a report. Much of what was in it, 
their own people had been telling them for months. I got some of the 
managers and some of the workforce round a table and pointed this out. It 
came down to credibility, and credibility was a function of how much they 
paid. Because I was expensive, management decided I must be right; for the 
same reason, the shop floor viewed me and what I was doing with suspicion.

From the management point of view, they perceived a risk in taking the 
advice of their own people, who may be driven by different agendas. Having 
an independent confirm it, made it OK. I suggested they address this by 
having better communication between management and shop floor. (although 
normally I am very averse to formal meetings...), setting aside mutual 
mistrust and being much more open. In that particular company, "joint policy 
meetings" were a big success.The management came to realise that they had 
some very capable people on board and the shop floor were happy to have a 
hand in shaping policy.

So whether I was right or wrong, things improved through my being there... 
:-)

>
> (For some values of "different people").

So I can exclude myself  from your comment? :-)  But actually, as I tried to 
show above, it is true. It just isn't necessarily a bad thing.

Pete.
```

<a id="t-c591d12e04"></a>
## Thread: Potential abolition of leap seconds - USNO impact survey — 3 msgs

### Potential abolition of leap seconds - USNO impact survey

- **From:** Robert Jones <rjones0@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-09-07T15:37:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.databases.ibm-db2
- **Message-ID:** `<4be9b283-e03e-4cf9-888f-d57bb98e1f55@z72g2000hsb.googlegroups.com>`

```
I thought the following link could be of interest:

The USNO is conducting a poll for the DoD about leap seconds.

http://tycho.usno.navy.mil/leap_second_poll.html

I note in the description that they do not recognize the possiblity of
having a leap-free broadcast time scale while still allowing leap
seconds in UTC.  Those really are two separate questions.

Posted by Steve Allen on the Listserve leap second discussion group,
which is email only

My own imprsssion is that most computer systems, especially
commercial, small business and personal, would find it much easier to
do without them. Navigation, astronomy and possibly telecommunications
applications may have rather more problems.

Robert
```

#### ↳ Re: Potential abolition of leap seconds - USNO impact survey

- **From:** Steve Allen <sla29970@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-09-07T22:37:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.databases.ibm-db2
- **Message-ID:** `<01d6ff2a-abe1-460b-8dbe-6b50864d0772@p31g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<4be9b283-e03e-4cf9-888f-d57bb98e1f55@z72g2000hsb.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Sep 7, 3:37 pm, Robert Jones <rjon...@hotmail.com> wrote:
> Posted by Steve Allen on the Listserve leap second discussion group,
> which is email only

The recent flow of LEAPSECS list is archived at
http://six.pairlist.net/pipermail/leapsecs/

> My own imprsssion is that most computer systems, especially
> commercial, small business and personal, would find it much easier to
> do without them. Navigation, astronomy and possibly telecommunications
> applications may have rather more problems.

Hugo Chavez didn't give Venezuela or the zoneinfo folks much
consideration when he moved their timezone by half an hour.  It would
be easier to do without changes in zoneinfo, but we don't get that
option.  It's a choice of how we value our descendants.  See
http://www.ucolick.org/~sla/leapsecs/deltat.html
```

<a id="t-19e2b9f7ad"></a>
## Thread: Laser Printing from MFCOBOL 4.5 — 13 msgs

### Laser Printing from MFCOBOL 4.5

- **From:** Nitin Kothari <nitkot@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-09-11T05:26:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a8711d3-2898-4129-90a1-695c8818b1e4@b2g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
Hi,

I am using MFCOBOL 4.5 (DOS/Novell Version) to develop commercial
application from last 18 years. I have done lot of development during
this tenure. Around 40 large softwares has been developed by me
individually. I am heading community of users in India. It works fine
on all windows operating system upto from 98 to VISTA.

I came to know about this group recently and happy to know that people
are actively contributing to keep COBOL alive. I always call myself as
"Its not COBOL Over.... but I am COBO Lover.....

Many of my customer has now bought laser printer, They would like to
print output on laser printer instead of DOT Matrix printer.

There may be print out of large sheet paper of 132 column, which needs
to be condensed from software to print correctly on laser printer,
which we can easily do on dot matrix printer.

Can any one share their knowledge on same?

Awaiting your response to the earliest.

Nitin Kothari
Mumbai, India
9867696515
```

#### ↳ Re: Laser Printing from MFCOBOL 4.5

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <mmattias@talsystems.com>
- **Date:** 2008-09-11T07:52:41-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Hw8yk.22771$89.9322@nlpi069.nbdc.sbc.com>`
- **References:** `<8a8711d3-2898-4129-90a1-695c8818b1e4@b2g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Nitin Kothari" <nitkot@gmail.com> wrote in message 
news:8a8711d3-2898-4129-90a1-695c8818b1e4@b2g2000prf.googlegroups.com...

> am using MFCOBOL 4.5 (DOS/Novell Version) ....
> Many of my customer has now bought laser printer, They would like to
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Can any one share their knowledge on same?

There should be an escape sequence you can send to the printer to throw it 
into "condensed" mode. This will be documented in the printer's owner's 
manual or reference manual. (Just like it works on your dot matrix).

That is, assuming this printer is NOT a 'Windows only' printer, in which 
case it will print only thru the Windows' driver. If a printer connects via 
USB port, good chance it's a "Windows-only" printer. Excellent chance 
actually. Um, make that  a virtual "lock."

If you can 'copy filename LPT1:'   from a command prompt and it prints (you 
may have to manually hit the 'form feed' button to eject the sheet), you are 
OK.

If this is a Windows'-only printer, you should be able to find a utilty 
which prints text files thru Windows facilities ....and you could call that 
as an external program from your COBOL program after generating your report 
to a disk file.  (I know such utilities exist.... eg in my "other language' 
group someone posted one for free use for others to use.  These utilities 
should offer an option for you to specify the font to be used, and selecting 
the correct font size/characteristics will handle the 'condensed' 
requirement.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Laser Printing from MFCOBOL 4.5

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2008-09-11T08:01:59-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v49ic4ls626o6fci1gqh32l1r75upltugb@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<8a8711d3-2898-4129-90a1-695c8818b1e4@b2g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <Hw8yk.22771$89.9322@nlpi069.nbdc.sbc.com>`

```

<mmattias@talsystems.com> wrote:

>That is, assuming this printer is NOT a 'Windows only' printer, in which 
>case it will print only thru the Windows' driver. If a printer connects via 
>USB port, good chance it's a "Windows-only" printer. Excellent chance 
>actually. Um, make that  a virtual "lock."

My wife's USB printer works similarly with Mac drivers.  I don't know
how it works with Linux.   I suppose the driver accepts the same
commands.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Laser Printing from MFCOBOL 4.5

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-09-11T11:36:09-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e3e2ef02-ebf5-4da4-8af8-5721fa699bfe@q5g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<8a8711d3-2898-4129-90a1-695c8818b1e4@b2g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <Hw8yk.22771$89.9322@nlpi069.nbdc.sbc.com>`

```
On Sep 12, 12:52 am, "Michael Mattias" <mmatt...@talsystems.com>
wrote:
> "Nitin Kothari" <nit...@gmail.com> wrote in message
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> actually. Um, make that  a virtual "lock."

Not true. There is no relationship between the printer being a 'GDI'
device and the interface used. GDI may be parallel port, USB or
network, but full PCL or PS printers will also use these.

In fact just yesterday I installed a new OKI B4600 PCL page printer
using USB.

> If you can 'copy filename LPT1:'   from a command prompt and it prints (you
> may have to manually hit the 'form feed' button to eject the sheet), you are
> OK.

It is unlikely that this will work without first setting the printer
to 'capture' LPT1.


> If this is a Windows'-only printer, you should be able to find a utilty
> which prints text files thru Windows facilities ....and you could call that
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> mmatt...@talsystems.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Laser Printing from MFCOBOL 4.5

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-09-11T12:02:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5f354996-ee8b-4a55-82d1-a7efebc5cd8a@n33g2000pri.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<8a8711d3-2898-4129-90a1-695c8818b1e4@b2g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Sep 12, 12:26 am, Nitin Kothari <nit...@gmail.com> wrote:
> Hi,
>
> I am using MFCOBOL 4.5 (DOS/Novell Version) to develop commercial

That is probably MicroSoft COBOL 4.5 which is a version of Microfocus
(MF) 3.x.

> application from last 18 years. I have done lot of development during
> this tenure. Around 40 large softwares has been developed by me
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> print output on laser printer instead of DOT Matrix printer.

If it is an HP or emulates it and supports PCL then the hex codes that
I would use are:

0x1B451B266C314F
0x1B2838551B2873307031362E363668382E3576307330623054
0x1B266C3844
0x1B266131354C

Note that 1B is escape and starts a string that ends with an upper
case letter that is the actual command. Lower case letters are also
commands but don't end the string. Escape indicated by @:

@E                      reset
@&l1O                   orientation landscape
@(8U                    can't remember
@(s0p16.66h8.5v0s0b0T   16.66 cpi 8.5 lpi fixed font
@&l8D                   top margin ??
@&a15L                  left margin 15

I have these strings in a control file that has different settings
depending on what the user requires.

If the printer is not PCL then it may have emulations that could be
used instead, such as IBM Proprinter.

The printer will need to be set to 'capture' a port that the program
can access, such as 'LPT1'. Some GDI drivers can, some will not. Most
real printer (non GDI) drivers can, after setting them to share, with

net use LPT1 \\server\sharename

(may not work on XP Home or other limited version, with Vista: don't
know, don't care, don't want to know)

You may need to ensure that the print driver has a 'raw' setting for
these to pass through.


> There may be print out of large sheet paper of 132 column, which needs
> to be condensed from software to print correctly on laser printer,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 9867696515
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Laser Printing from MFCOBOL 4.5

- **From:** Rene_Surop <infodynamics_ph@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2008-09-11T19:01:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a609effa-1f92-4417-9cc6-8d8aca25fd15@n33g2000pri.googlegroups.com>`

```
>
> > I am using MFCOBOL 4.5 (DOS/Novell Version) to develop commercial
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If you are using MF NetExpress devtool, then you can use several
options (refer to URL link) from their sample applications.

http://supportline.microfocus.com/examplesandutilities/nesamp.asp#Printing

For Microfocus support, the above site is a great help.


Rene
```

#### ↳ Re: Laser Printing from MFCOBOL 4.5

- **From:** Alain Reymond <arwebmail@skynet.be>
- **Date:** 2008-09-12T10:18:41+02:00

- **Message-ID:** `<48ca2636$0$2865$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<8a8711d3-2898-4129-90a1-695c8818b1e4@b2g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<8a8711d3-2898-4129-90a1-695c8818b1e4@b2g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
Nitin,

An interessant alternative might be RPV Report from RPVSoftware.
Otherwise, you will have to deal with PCL codes.

Regards

Alain
```

#### ↳ Re: Laser Printing from MFCOBOL 4.5

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-09-16T10:41:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rrednS2ah6RYTlLVnZ2dnUVZ_umdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<8a8711d3-2898-4129-90a1-695c8818b1e4@b2g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
Nitin Kothari wrote:
> Hi,
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> 9867696515

You have two problems. Laser (page) printers have lots of additional 
commands that are necessary: Font, font size, margins, page length, stroke 
weight, lines-per-inch, characters-per-inch, paper size, tray, etc. About 
100 bytes. Fortunately, these commands can be stored in a file which you 
must send to the printer every time you open the print file.

Your second problem is printing to a port other than LPT1(2), especially XP, 
2000, and Vista which do not allow "Capture Printer Port."

There are techniques for so doing on Windows machines involving pooling the 
printer port. One can be found here:

http://members.shaw.ca/bsanders/printfromdos.htm

Possibly the easiest is the program RPV (Report Program Viewer) already 
mentioned if you can still find the free version. RPV works as follows: You 
must direct all your printing to a file at a specific location, instead of 
the printer. RPV (running in the background) continually monitors this 
location and, when it finds a file in the folder, grabs the file and sends 
it to the Windows print spooler.

Of course this presumes you were foresighted enough to put all your printing 
commands in a sub-program so you don't have to make thousands of changes to 
hundreds of programs...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Laser Printing from MFCOBOL 4.5

- **From:** epc8@juno.com

```
> commands that are necessary: Font, font size, margins, page length, stroke
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…

A Google search does not find RPV. But this might work. See

http://www.lerup.com/printfile/

The online docs say that PrintFile also acts as a printer spooler for
files in a particular directory. It also list command line options for
input. YMMV. I have NOT tried or installed this program.

- e
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Laser Printing from MFCOBOL 4.5

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-09-18T10:19:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6LmdnZhwvp_r7E_VnZ2dnUVZ_jqdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<8a8711d3-2898-4129-90a1-695c8818b1e4@b2g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <rrednS2ah6RYTlLVnZ2dnUVZ_umdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <4639e8e3-f841-467c-87f6-7fe277d980fb@x41g2000hsb.googlegroups.com>`

```
epc8@juno.com wrote:
> On Sep 16, 11:41 am, "HeyBub" <hey...@NOSPAMgmail.com> wrote:
>> Nitin Kothari wrote:
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think I found the old RPV here:

http://www.rpvsoftware.com/downloads.php

We used and distributed Version 2.00; maybe the available V2.2 will work.

Still, the concept is reasonable. A program that, once started, polls a 
specific folder for any new files every ten or thirty seconds. If a file is 
found, grab the file an send it to a printer defined at startup (or via 
command line variables).

Meanwhile, other ad hoc programs are diverting their print jobs to a file in 
the specified folder.

If someone wants to try RPV 2.0, email me privately and I'll send along the 
copy I have (orginally marked 'freely distributable').

As an aside, this illustrates the wonderfulness of putting I/O in a 
sub-program. In our case, it was a, maybe, ten-line change to optionally 
divert printing to a file instead of LPT1. Re-link of 80 programs and we 
were good to go.
```

```
I am an ardent supporter of tiered architecture, with separation layers 
(interfaces).

There are SO many benefits in doing this which are simply not apparent when 
you first do it. After you implement a few systems using this architecture, 
and some years go by, you suddenly find benefits which you couldn't envision 
crawling out of the woodwork. Swap the data resource to the latest 
technology (maybe even a new technology...), but don't change the business 
logic...? Easy. Want to move from green screens to GUI? Easy. The list goes 
on.

I'm sold.

Pete.
```

<a id="t-d4083916ab"></a>
## Thread: COBOL Subroutine Linkage Section Question — 17 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### COBOL Subroutine Linkage Section Question

- **From:** OppThumb <calahanj@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-09-12T12:37:16-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ebe0c7bb-0c71-49a0-9e46-fc64d6dec9a0@z66g2000hsc.googlegroups.com>`

```
To All,

I work on a mainframe system for which I'm developing code to analyze
files and develop distributions by values in individual fields in
those files. I want to set up a call to a subroutine program that will
look something like this:

CALL subroutine USING field
                                    field-length
                                    distribution-table

The idea is that each row in the table will have a unique value,
followed by a tally. New values will have new rows populated (not
added - each table will be predefined to have a maximum of 50 rows),
while existing rows will only have the tally incremented. From this
you can see the tables will not occupy very much in the way of
storage, no more than a few KB at the most.

Here's the catch -- I want to call the same subroutine program
multiple times for any of a number of fields and associated tables,
some or all of which will have varying field lengths (and by
extension, the table rows will be of varying length as well). Using
some sort of DEPENDING ON clause won't do the trick because I already
know how many rows will exist, just not the length of each row.

I can pass in the field length (as seen above) and from there
dynamically work with the values in the subroutine. What I want to
know is, how do I code the Linkage Section statements? Incidentally,
I'm calling the COBOL program from an Easytrieve program, so I'm not
sure that something like address pointers would work for me. Any and
all help would be appreciated.

Thanks,

John
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Subroutine Linkage Section Question

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2008-09-12T13:57:09-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c5ilc4hitg0iam3i1tskp50942m0940grd@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ebe0c7bb-0c71-49a0-9e46-fc64d6dec9a0@z66g2000hsc.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Fri, 12 Sep 2008 12:37:16 -0700 (PDT), OppThumb
<calahanj@gmail.com> wrote:

>Here's the catch -- I want to call the same subroutine program
>multiple times for any of a number of fields and associated tables,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>know how many rows will exist, just not the length of each row.

I don't see why DEPENDING ON won't do the trick.

>I can pass in the field length (as seen above) and from there
>dynamically work with the values in the subroutine. What I want to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>all help would be appreciated.

It looks like you're using an IBM mainframe.   So your CoBOL program
will have something like this:
                                              
 LINKAGE SECTION.                             
 01  PASSED-RECORD.                           
     05  PASSED-LENGTH     PIC 9(4)  COMP.    
     05  PASSED-DATA.
          10 ...

That length field will be populated by the system.                
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qmilc4pupdimva534elo3llm5ke5bkhjjl@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ebe0c7bb-0c71-49a0-9e46-fc64d6dec9a0@z66g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <c5ilc4hitg0iam3i1tskp50942m0940grd@4ax.com>`

```
On Fri, 12 Sep 2008 13:57:09 -0600, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>It looks like you're using an IBM mainframe.   So your CoBOL program
>will have something like this:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>That length field will be populated by the system.  

If you have a maximum size, you might consider just creating a linkage
area that big, then manipulate it however you wish.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<34bd5ee1-0687-41ba-b764-9309f05754bf@m45g2000hsb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<ebe0c7bb-0c71-49a0-9e46-fc64d6dec9a0@z66g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <c5ilc4hitg0iam3i1tskp50942m0940grd@4ax.com> <qmilc4pupdimva534elo3llm5ke5bkhjjl@4ax.com>`

```
On Sep 12, 4:03 pm, Howard Brazee <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
> On Fri, 12 Sep 2008 13:57:09 -0600, Howard Brazee <how...@brazee.net>
> wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> - James Madison

To answer an earlier question, yes, it's an IBM mainframe.

I thought about sending it through as just one big area, then chopping
it up as needed, based on the record length (reference modification,
etc.). I won't know my maximum from one file to the next, though -- if
I end up with a definition in the subroutine program that's not big
enough for what's handed down to it, is there any way I can
dynamically point beyond the end of what's defined in my Linkage
Section? I can't say I know, but COBOL does some interesting things
these days.

Thanks again.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Subroutine Linkage Section Question

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-09-13T00:16:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gaf0p2$5eo$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<ebe0c7bb-0c71-49a0-9e46-fc64d6dec9a0@z66g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <c5ilc4hitg0iam3i1tskp50942m0940grd@4ax.com> <qmilc4pupdimva534elo3llm5ke5bkhjjl@4ax.com> <34bd5ee1-0687-41ba-b764-9309f05754bf@m45g2000hsb.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <34bd5ee1-0687-41ba-b764-9309f05754bf@m45g2000hsb.googlegroups.com>,
OppThumb  <calahanj@gmail.com> wrote:
>On Sep 12, 4:03�pm, Howard Brazee <how...@brazee.net> wrote:

[snip]

>> If you have a maximum size, you might consider just creating a linkage
>> area that big, then manipulate it however you wish.

[snip]

>I thought about sending it through as just one big area, then chopping
>it up as needed, based on the record length (reference modification,
>etc.).

As a subtle suggestion to indicate my opinion of this possiblity... NO, 
NO, THRICE NO.  Some posters to this newsgroup get their panties in a 
bunch about REDEFINES or 88s; it is my experience that while reference 
modification is a *most* valuable addition to the language I have seen it 
used in fashions for 'just this once' that cause difficulties in 
maintenance... compare:

IF CUST-HDR-REC AND LOCAL-ZIP-CODE
    PERFORM A17258-STATE-TAX-RTN  THRU  A17258-EX.

... with ...

If WS-Cust-Info-Rec(1:1) = '1' and WS-Cust-ZipCd(1:2) = '02'
    Perform State-Tax-Rtn
End-If

>I won't know my maximum from one file to the next, though -- if
>I end up with a definition in the subroutine program that's not big
>enough for what's handed down to it, is there any way I can
>dynamically point beyond the end of what's defined in my Linkage
>Section?

If you end up with a definition in the subroutine program that's not big 
enough then I would say that the design is need of revisiting.  Look 
through what you have and need for now... and double that amount of space.

If double what you need now exceeds the amount of space that can be passed 
by a CALL... then redesign is most *definitely* in need of revisiting.

>I can't say I know, but COBOL does some interesting things
>these days.

COBOL has always done some 'interesting things'... have you ever dealt 
with a hastily-implemented 66-level at 2:am?  Have you ever found that 
adding 'just one more file' creates a load module so big that it won't fit 
into core?  How about finding out the length of a variable-length record 
by referring to... oh, folks don't do that any more, do they?

The challenge is in making the code simple.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Subroutine Linkage Section Question

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@Nospam.neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2008-09-13T11:11:33-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uolnc4dvee7184e3dq2ug5v5hoq5hmu3ae@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ebe0c7bb-0c71-49a0-9e46-fc64d6dec9a0@z66g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <c5ilc4hitg0iam3i1tskp50942m0940grd@4ax.com> <qmilc4pupdimva534elo3llm5ke5bkhjjl@4ax.com> <34bd5ee1-0687-41ba-b764-9309f05754bf@m45g2000hsb.googlegroups.com> <gaf0p2$5eo$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Sat, 13 Sep 2008 00:16:34 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>In article <34bd5ee1-0687-41ba-b764-9309f05754bf@m45g2000hsb.googlegroups.com>,
>OppThumb  <calahanj@gmail.com> wrote:
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
>DD

The OP mentioned he was calling the COBOL subroutine from an
Easytrieve.  I'm assuming you have multiple Easytrieves to handle the
different size files.  The only suggestion I might make since you want
to have only 1 COBOL subroutine is to code a 32K size area in your
COBOL and Easytrieve modules.  Populate the 32K area with your table
and send it and the "real" length to the subroutine.

If your files or tables ever exceed 32k obviously you'd have to change
it in all of the Easytrieves and the subroutine but it would be a
simple 1 line change everywhere.

Regards,
 
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

"He who laughs last thinks slowest."
-- Steven Wright
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Subroutine Linkage Section Question

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-09-14T06:41:36+00:00
