###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Moving Microfocus 3.1 to a better laptop

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-09-20T22:41:46+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6jk2bcF3ngg4U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<7300a46c-2e0a-4f13-86d0-bb4d9f7448a2@i24g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <WAHAk.13407$wr1.9652@newsfe02.iad> <6jj5ltF3jh3vU1@mid.individual.net> <48d4ac1c$0$9711$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net>`

```


"Paul H" <NoSpamphobergNoSpam@att.net> wrote in message 
news:48d4ac1c$0$9711$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net...
> Hi Pete.  Sounds like you know your way around this issue.  Am I stuck?

Let's hope not.

I've checked out how their Licensing works and it is similar to what I 
recall of Fujitsu, but not quite as stupid. (You don't need to transfer to a 
floppy...maybe you don't with Fujitsu these days either... let's hope so.) 
Also, MF have documented the process extremely well.

As the version running on your original machine is still licensed, you can 
legitimately revoke that license and transfer it to the new machine. Your 
question was: "How do I do the second step?"

Here's the answer: 
http://www.microfocus.com/000/RevokingLicensesonWindows_tcm21-7985.pdf

I found this to be a very well written document. It is clear and uses 
screenshots to good advantage. You should read it through then do it.

Basically there are two parts:

1. You get a Revoke Key string (issued by their server), using the key you 
have.
2. You use this key to get a new key for the new machine.

As they explain it...
"Record and save the Revoke Key string for submission with your request for 
a new Response Key to unlock your product. Use the Request/Obtain License 
selection in the Self-Service menu of the SupportLine web site to request 
the replacement Response Key. Alternatively, an e-mail message containing 
your company name and location, your product serial number, the Request Key, 
and the Revoke Key strings can be sent to URK@Microfocus.com. In either 
case, an e-mail will be returned containing your new Response Key which is 
used to unlock the product."

I think they could have put the link into the above, but then, it could be 
subject to change. It's already out of date because there is no 
"Self-Service" option on the site.  Anyway, I ran it down for you... 
http://supportline.microfocus.com/license_request.asp?prod=NX&type=Reqrev

This form works for most License-related activities. Just use the "Request 
type" box near the bottom to get a Revoke Key, then use that key with the 
same form but different Request Type, to get the Response key you need.

Hope this helps.

[Aside to Jimmy]: You should be able to use this form to get a replacement 
key for your corrupted one. You probably have your original key noted 
somewhere... I'd revoke that, then use the Revoke Key to get a new key, and 
re-install. Actually, thinking about how the process works, if you are 
re-installing onto the same machine, the same original key should work fine. 
You don't need to get it re-issued. DONT re-install until you are absolutely 
sure your machine is clean... :-) [/Aside]

I used MF exclusively, just as you did for DOS based applications, and 
Windows 3.1 when it arrived. You probably remember PANELs and early DIALOG? 
Despite the ups and downs of the company over the years, their products 
were, and are, generally excellent. I was very keen to get into OO COBOL as 
soon as I heard it was becoming available (but I had to save up... :-)). I 
was just about to purchase VISOC (Visual Object COBOL) when they withdrew 
all support for it in favour of the NetExpress product, thereby shafting 
everyone who had bought it. At the same time Fujitsu offered a FREE  OO 
COBOL compiler

> I've been using MicroFocus on PCs since it was COBOL2 in the early 80's. 
> I
> recently ran across COBOL2 manuals in my garage - I finally threw them 
> away.

I'm keeping mine for a project I have in mind when I retire... :-)

> I'm responsible for hundreds of work stations running legal copies of
> WorkBench.  More recently, as the applications have moved to newer 
> versions
> of NetExpress, end users are now paying run time licenses.  I've helped
> MicroFocus make many thousands of $.

Bet you're sorry now... so are all those people sandbagged into paying for 
the right to run something they already bought (or thought they did...)

> Now that I'm retired and living
> frugally, they want me to buy a new copy - to bring my maintenance
> up-to-date would actually cost more.

No, Paul, they will stand by legitimately licensed products people have 
bought. You should be able to revoke your key and get a new one. Of course, 
they'd PREFER it if you bought another copy...:-)

If you find they won't revoke your key, THEN you have a right to hit the War 
Path. Let's cross that bridge if/when we come to it. I don't think it will 
happen.

>Maybe my only option is to communicate
> with someone at the VP level or higher.

Well, at the moment they have a pretty useful guy running the show, so we 
could hope he is making his influence felt in the attitude and style of the 
people reporting to him. I wouldn't bother him with this. While it is really 
important to you, he has other priorities. This should be an option of 
absolutely last resort. As for getting ot a VP, usually difficult unless you 
know somebody, and really only an option of last resort. These are very busy 
people; give their assigned managers a chance to sort it first...

> Any easier suggestions?  TIA, Paul

Yes. See above.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Moving Microfocus 3.1 to a better laptop

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Rene_Surop <infodynamics_ph@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2008-09-20T08:47:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b81fc059-66f2-4ec7-916d-3ea22aab9d8b@w1g2000prk.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<7300a46c-2e0a-4f13-86d0-bb4d9f7448a2@i24g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <WAHAk.13407$wr1.9652@newsfe02.iad> <6jj5ltF3jh3vU1@mid.individual.net> <48d4ac1c$0$9711$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <6jk2bcF3ngg4U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
All of us using MF NetExpress v3.1 experienced "re-installation"
eventually.... since PC models manufactured that year is probably not
working (memory, keyboard, harddisk). I have the same problem with my
'crashed' harddisk and eventually my laptop retired.

Acquired my MF NetExpress copy thru consultancy trade-in. Meaning I
have to teach MF NetExpress to MF affiliate clients (for free, with
only training expenses) and in return they (the MF affiliate) will
give me an evaluation copy. Enjoyed it for years..... BUT when v3.1
became obsolete, nah. I did not get any support afterwards. Who will
give support to somebody just explaining how to program in Cobol using
MF product anyway?

They SELL copies of MF new versions now to banks, insurance companies
or Accenture (for their Accounting software Alnova created in MF) here
in the Philippines. Hope they're gaining. Did not involved myself into
MF product after that.

Found a solution of my own though, installing the old MF NetExpress
v3.1.... through determination and persistency, and it worked.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Moving Microfocus 3.1 to a better laptop

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Paul H" <NoSpamphobergNoSpam@att.net>
- **Date:** 2008-09-20T17:35:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<48d57aac$0$9722$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net>`
- **References:** `<7300a46c-2e0a-4f13-86d0-bb4d9f7448a2@i24g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <WAHAk.13407$wr1.9652@newsfe02.iad> <6jj5ltF3jh3vU1@mid.individual.net> <48d4ac1c$0$9711$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <6jk2bcF3ngg4U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete, you expended a lot of effort on my behalf.  I appreciate it.  My worst 
scenario is, of course, to disable the old laptop, then be unable to get the 
new one licensed.  Do you think that if I create a restore point on the old 
laptop before I revoke the key, I could go back if I get into trouble? 
Again, TIA, Paul

==================
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:6jk2bcF3ngg4U1@mid.individual.net...


"Paul H" <NoSpamphobergNoSpam@att.net> wrote in message
news:48d4ac1c$0$9711$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net...
> Hi Pete.  Sounds like you know your way around this issue.  Am I stuck?

Let's hope not.

I've checked out how their Licensing works and it is similar to what I
recall of Fujitsu, but not quite as stupid. (You don't need to transfer to a
floppy...maybe you don't with Fujitsu these days either... let's hope so.)
Also, MF have documented the process extremely well.

As the version running on your original machine is still licensed, you can
legitimately revoke that license and transfer it to the new machine. Your
question was: "How do I do the second step?"

Here's the answer:
http://www.microfocus.com/000/RevokingLicensesonWindows_tcm21-7985.pdf

I found this to be a very well written document. It is clear and uses
screenshots to good advantage. You should read it through then do it.

Basically there are two parts:

1. You get a Revoke Key string (issued by their server), using the key you
have.
2. You use this key to get a new key for the new machine.

As they explain it...
"Record and save the Revoke Key string for submission with your request for
a new Response Key to unlock your product. Use the Request/Obtain License
selection in the Self-Service menu of the SupportLine web site to request
the replacement Response Key. Alternatively, an e-mail message containing
your company name and location, your product serial number, the Request Key,
and the Revoke Key strings can be sent to URK@Microfocus.com. In either
case, an e-mail will be returned containing your new Response Key which is
used to unlock the product."

I think they could have put the link into the above, but then, it could be
subject to change. It's already out of date because there is no
"Self-Service" option on the site.  Anyway, I ran it down for you...
http://supportline.microfocus.com/license_request.asp?prod=NX&type=Reqrev

This form works for most License-related activities. Just use the "Request
type" box near the bottom to get a Revoke Key, then use that key with the
same form but different Request Type, to get the Response key you need.

Hope this helps.

[Aside to Jimmy]: You should be able to use this form to get a replacement
key for your corrupted one. You probably have your original key noted
somewhere... I'd revoke that, then use the Revoke Key to get a new key, and
re-install. Actually, thinking about how the process works, if you are
re-installing onto the same machine, the same original key should work fine.
You don't need to get it re-issued. DONT re-install until you are absolutely
sure your machine is clean... :-) [/Aside]

I used MF exclusively, just as you did for DOS based applications, and
Windows 3.1 when it arrived. You probably remember PANELs and early DIALOG?
Despite the ups and downs of the company over the years, their products
were, and are, generally excellent. I was very keen to get into OO COBOL as
soon as I heard it was becoming available (but I had to save up... :-)). I
was just about to purchase VISOC (Visual Object COBOL) when they withdrew
all support for it in favour of the NetExpress product, thereby shafting
everyone who had bought it. At the same time Fujitsu offered a FREE  OO
COBOL compiler

> I've been using MicroFocus on PCs since it was COBOL2 in the early 80's.
> I
> recently ran across COBOL2 manuals in my garage - I finally threw them
> away.

I'm keeping mine for a project I have in mind when I retire... :-)

> I'm responsible for hundreds of work stations running legal copies of
> WorkBench.  More recently, as the applications have moved to newer
> versions
> of NetExpress, end users are now paying run time licenses.  I've helped
> MicroFocus make many thousands of $.

Bet you're sorry now... so are all those people sandbagged into paying for
the right to run something they already bought (or thought they did...)

> Now that I'm retired and living
> frugally, they want me to buy a new copy - to bring my maintenance
> up-to-date would actually cost more.

No, Paul, they will stand by legitimately licensed products people have
bought. You should be able to revoke your key and get a new one. Of course,
they'd PREFER it if you bought another copy...:-)

If you find they won't revoke your key, THEN you have a right to hit the War
Path. Let's cross that bridge if/when we come to it. I don't think it will
happen.

>Maybe my only option is to communicate
> with someone at the VP level or higher.

Well, at the moment they have a pretty useful guy running the show, so we
could hope he is making his influence felt in the attitude and style of the
people reporting to him. I wouldn't bother him with this. While it is really
important to you, he has other priorities. This should be an option of
absolutely last resort. As for getting ot a VP, usually difficult unless you
know somebody, and really only an option of last resort. These are very busy
people; give their assigned managers a chance to sort it first...

> Any easier suggestions?  TIA, Paul

Yes. See above.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Moving Microfocus 3.1 to a better laptop

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-09-21T11:38:50+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6jlfs9F3ukvgU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<7300a46c-2e0a-4f13-86d0-bb4d9f7448a2@i24g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <WAHAk.13407$wr1.9652@newsfe02.iad> <6jj5ltF3jh3vU1@mid.individual.net> <48d4ac1c$0$9711$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <6jk2bcF3ngg4U1@mid.individual.net> <48d57aac$0$9722$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net>`

```
"Paul H" <NoSpamphobergNoSpam@att.net> wrote in message 
news:48d57aac$0$9722$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net...
> Pete, you expended a lot of effort on my behalf.  I appreciate it.

No problem. It wasn't much effort really... around an hour, including 
reading the document. I'm recovering from a rotten cold at the moment so it 
isn't too hard to sit in the sunshine and learn something. :-)

> My worst
> scenario is, of course, to disable the old laptop, then be unable to get 
> the
> new one licensed.

Yes, I understand.

It is a remote possibility. If the MF server revoked your old key it will 
have marked that there is still 1 licence available on your account.

You should therefore be able to re-instate the key on the original machine 
if you need to.


>Do you think that if I create a restore point on the old
> laptop before I revoke the key, I could go back if I get into trouble?

While you're doing this exercise and until you have everything safely 
transferred, it wouldn't hurt to have a system restore point. However, once 
you have revoked the licence, I'd be surprised if running a system restore 
could make it valid again. System restore is more concerned with OS software 
than application software although the snapshot (dunno why they call it 
that; they are huge...) will include previous registry settings that may put 
applications into a previous state.

> Again, TIA, Paul
>
You're welcome :-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Moving Microfocus 3.1 to a better laptop

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2008-09-20T16:19:59-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hGeBk.15988$rV4.14867@newsfe03.iad>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<6jj5ltF3jh3vU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<7300a46c-2e0a-4f13-86d0-bb4d9f7448a2@i24g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <WAHAk.13407$wr1.9652@newsfe02.iad> <6jj5ltF3jh3vU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> Hi Jimmy,
> 
> I was so shocked and horrified by your experience (and it is a signal
> warning to PaulH to be very careful before trying to move stuff)
> that I'd really like to help. Some thoughts below...

Not quite as bad as it sounds. I could have looked at the M/F site to 
give Paul references, but on the spur of the moment googled and found at 
least one page, (maybe there were more). Thought - this will give him 
several lead-ins to M/F and he might pick up on something I was unaware 
of - to the point, I was not aware of the 'Revoke' feature.

Paul - If just dabbling with Net Express, you may not need the SP1 
(Service Pack) updates. I think the compiler is fairly SOLID and if the 
fixpacks did anything material, it was probably to add ons outside of 
the compiler itself.

Pete - Somebody, not associated with the licensing, has already offered 
to follow-up on my key in so far as he can. The Incident Report goes 
back to mid-April. Other than no reply, not their fault it wasn't 
concluded. In my declining enthusiasm for programming and having had hip 
surgery May 2007, I thought "Sod it! I'm going to spend this year in the 
garden". Needless to say, couldn't do much last year so both physically 
and mentally it was a good exercise I've been enjoying, and continue to 
enjoy until everything gets zapped with frost in late September, 
(weather is gorgeous today).

Regrettably, think I'm going to have to reformat my XP and re-load 
everything. Uggh ! I'll do an 'OT' on the re-install which answers some 
of the points you made.

Let's use a Pareto analysis; there are 99.8% good guys using their 
compiler, so just to ensure 0.2% bad guys don't get away with a freebie 
version, penalize and irritate the 99.8% with License Keys.

There are currently very few messages in the M/F Forum on a daily basis 
- I've been wondering why for some time now - dotNet perhaps ? From a 
couple of messages, and I'm sure originally he was enthused, Rene 
doesn't sound like a happy camper.

I want to get back and answer John with his Easytrieve problem. I get 
the feeling he is young and 'drowning' a bit.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Moving Microfocus 3.1 to a better laptop

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-09-21T13:32:43+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6jlmhrF3uguhU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<7300a46c-2e0a-4f13-86d0-bb4d9f7448a2@i24g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <WAHAk.13407$wr1.9652@newsfe02.iad> <6jj5ltF3jh3vU1@mid.individual.net> <hGeBk.15988$rV4.14867@newsfe03.iad>`

```


"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:hGeBk.15988$rV4.14867@newsfe03.iad...
<snip>
>
> Pete - Somebody, not associated with the licensing, has already offered to 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I thought "Sod it! I'm going to spend this year in the garden".

<non-COBOL related text.>
Good for you, Jimmy. I have a number of friends here who've had hip or knee 
replacements and it gives them a whole new lease of life. Be careful with 
the squatting, if you're gardening, (but I'm sure they've told you what and 
what not to do :-)). Personally, I'm a hopeless gardener and employ someone 
to do it. From where I'm sitting I can see the first of the roses and the 
last of the orchids, the nectarine and peach trees have shed their glorious 
blossoms and decided it is now Summer, and there are various wildflowers 
making an appearance. It is really enjoyable to look at.

I enjoy cooking and the dining (and wining...) experience. At the moment I 
can pick oranges through my kitchen window :-)... it makes me smile every 
time I see the laden trees. I picked 4 this morning (through the window, 
just because I can... :-)) and juiced them, delicious.

My neighbours have a vegetable garden as well as their citrus but they don't 
have apples, nectarines, or peaches, so we sometimes trade :-)

Another of my friends grows the most unbelievable grapes and has her house 
virtually covered in them around February. This year she's decided to make 
wine... I need to figure out something I can offer her... :-)

>Needless to say, couldn't do much last year so both physically and mentally 
>it was a good exercise I've been enjoying, and continue to enjoy until 
>everything gets zapped with frost in late September, (weather is gorgeous 
>today).

I remember the winters are pretty fierce in Calgary. Doesn't this kill off 
most of the garden, or do you protect the plants? Do you plant species that 
can survive? I'm interested to know how gardening in a climate like yours 
differs from gardening in a sub-tropical climate like mine.

</non-COBOL related text>

>
> Regrettably, think I'm going to have to reformat my XP and re-load 
> everything. Uggh ! I'll do an 'OT' on the re-install which answers some of 
> the points you made.

I don't understand 'OT'...

Never mind. While this is drastic (it would be unthinkable for me; my 
notebook has 1.7 million files on it...:-)) it is definitely the way to 
ensure peace of mind.

Once you have the clean install it will be flooded by windows update 
requests if you have Automatic Update turned on. I'd keep it off until you 
have a successful manual update of SP 3 (see previous post). After that it 
should be OK.

Good luck and sing out if you need help.

>
> Let's use a Pareto analysis; there are 99.8% good guys using their 
> compiler, so just to ensure 0.2% bad guys don't get away with a freebie 
> version, penalize and irritate the 99.8% with License Keys.

Yep. That's about the size of it. However, they are unlikely to change it 
now, and to be fair, it isn't as bad as the early Fujitsu one I described. 
Still academic though. People who want to will circumvent it, and the people 
who want to, are the people it was supposed to protect against.

>
> There are currently very few messages in the M/F Forum on a daily basis - 
> I've been wondering why for some time now - dotNet perhaps ?

You mean the MF .NET compiler? I'm sure many businesses will take this up, 
believing they HAVE to if they want to play COBOL on the Elysian fields of 
the .NET framework. While it is good to have if you can afford it, and are 
committed to continuing development in COBOL, it is really not needed if you 
are planning to move away from COBOL, and only want your legacy to run on 
.NET, alongside new development in new languages.

A comprehensive example of this (with full source code) will be available in 
the coming week.


> From a couple of messages, and I'm sure originally he was enthused, Rene 
> doesn't sound like a happy camper.
>

I don't blame him.

> I want to get back and answer John with his Easytrieve problem. I get the 
> feeling he is young and 'drowning' a bit.
>

Easytrieve? Watch it Jimmy, you may find yourself working on a mainframe 
site :-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Moving Microfocus 3.1 to a better laptop

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2008-09-21T20:07:15-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<k5DBk.7849$tp1.7590@newsfe06.iad>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<6jlmhrF3uguhU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<7300a46c-2e0a-4f13-86d0-bb4d9f7448a2@i24g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <WAHAk.13407$wr1.9652@newsfe02.iad> <6jj5ltF3jh3vU1@mid.individual.net> <hGeBk.15988$rV4.14867@newsfe03.iad> <6jlmhrF3uguhU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:

Still NON-COBOL related, so folks ignore if you like.

> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
> news:hGeBk.15988$rV4.14867@newsfe03.iad...
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> making an appearance. It is really enjoyable to look at.

Hip wise I was lucky, almost like a non-event. Something like a 6 - 8
inch incision. (A house-visiting threpaist told me I could see vidoes
where the surgeon converts to butcher mode to saw out the defunct bone.
No thanks very bloody much I don't watch any of the medic programs on
TV; just occasionally Black Adder's sidekick, Hugh Laurie in 'House').

I hardly have a scar now. My female surgeon, Dr. Maureen O'Brien, (god
bless the little Irish darlin' - well a generation or two back), uses
her favoured technique. I still call 'em sticthes but they've become
sutures.  She uses a continuous piece of steel (?) thread and when it
comes to taking it out you just snip both ends and gently pull the
thread - no twitches from patient.

You'd think I would have suffered from that incision; not at all. The
pain came from re-discovering all those nerves in my body which are
buddy-buddy with the hip. Pain-wise the nurses, (an absolutely dedicated
group of people), came around asking, "How is you pain on a scale of 1
to 10 ?". Invariably I answerd "Three". "Do you want one or two Tylenol
3's ?". I could cope with just one; the second tablet would have been in
reserve if I needed it.

I'm happily doggy-walking again' deliberately attempting to stand
upright, (Military - "Chin and stomach in, chest out !"). I don't have
the old 100% balance in the left leg and I'm in tourble when out if I
need to pick something up - like doggy doo - NO ! not with my hands; I
pick it up with a plasitc bag. So I still use a walking cane, using it
as a pivot and swing my left leg up behind me and then bend over.

Eileen incidentaly has had both knees done. Bad reaction to the second
abut two years after the first; must have picked up a bug in hospital;
it didn't healing properly asnd wasa weeping.  She also got something
called Baker's Cyst. Heard of it ? Occurs bewhind the knee and not much
than can do but let it cure itself.

Met a Lady only last night. Her dad (93) was from Yeovil, Somerset and
she said he worked in a laboratory. Well about the only industry in the
town when he would have been there waas glove making. But she's written
to ask him - was it the Aplin and Barrett Laborartory, makers of St.
Ivel Lactic Cheese and St. Ivel paste, (pates). Part of Unigate, that's
how I ended up in Yeovil just prior to switching to EDP.

After the long blurb about her dad - she had a very bad reaction to knee
surgery. She was in constant pain and they kept feeding her morphine.
Turns out she was allergic to morphine - you can imagine the reaction.
> 
> I enjoy cooking and the dining (and wining...) experience. At the moment I 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> differs from gardening in a sub-tropical climate like mine.

Well certainly we can't grow any of the delectables that you've
mentioned. But here goes - fruitwise - apple trees - was a very modest
size apple and quickly turned to a pulp texture, so we dug it up. I did
grow black and red curreants and goosberries. Snag is, from a family of
four I was the only one that did the picking; the other three helped eat
them. We got delicious black currants but the plants tend to get a
mildew. Red currants were OK, but tastewise nothing like the sharp taste
of black currants.

Had one of my beloved dogs creamated years ago and half of her is
scattered at El Poca Heights in Kananaskis country, a winding drive up
some 6 kms of gravel. Up at that height goosberries grow wild. My own
goosberries, the size of the knob on the end of a ball-point pen - just
plain bloody pathetic. I did a number when I went back to UK in 2000,
and before going to the COBOL 'thing' at Newbury, stayed at Englefield
Green just above the RAF Memorial and Runnymede, our friend offered us
appple pie or gooseberry pie. I was like one of Snow White's Seven
Dwarfs, "GOOSEBERRY PIE !". I devoured the whole bloody thing by myself
while the others looked on in amusment.

Back to my own goosberries. I loved her dearly, (now dead), but my
sister-in-law was watching me pick gooseberries, and me enduring the
prickles, she casually said, "We leave those on the bushes back home".
Her holiday eventually finished and when she was gone, I thought "Sod
you !" and dug the bloody things up !

Raspberries - just great. This was a poor year for gardening much too
cold/coll in the spring/summer start. For no apparent reason some plants
have died off so we only got about a 25% return. 2006 and 2007, I kid
you not, we had raspberry canes which were 8 feet tall.

Blackberries - a disaster in Calgary though they grow in BC, and the
Island for sure, and probably back East in Ontario. We planted a
domestic plant which threw out one branch, but the winter killed that
off and second year you are back to the plant attempting to grow again.

Both in BC and Ontario, in additioin to apples they grow numerous other
fruits. Around southern Ontario near Niagra they have vineyards. My
daughter's kid's gave here a bottle of Ice Wine for Mother's Day. The
grapes are picked in late October after the first frosts. She and I
shared the bottle - nectar.

Trees - well firs of course, and aspens which books claim are actually
more numerous than fir trees in Canada. In our garden we have several
spruce, weeping birch (the white speckled bark with elgant branches
hanging down to the ground), rowan/mountain ash - the latter attracts a
flock of bohemian waxwings who shred the red berries from the tree in
late winter. Lilacs of course.

Flower wise - a bit of a challenge. Tried both rosemary and lavendar by
keeping them covered with polystreen pots from the auntumn until at
least mid-May. After a couple of years they succumbed. The bulb family
does well, both daffodills and tulips. We don't bother too much with the
latter, deer eat the tulip heads and ignore the daffodills; they come up
from Fish Creek Provincial Park, about ten minutes walk - and its the
largest urban park in Canada; not manicured and left as is).

Perennials - no correct that - Biennials like Canterbury Bells and
English Wallflowers require nursing; cover them with leaves in the
autumn once the young plants have grown and then plant them out in the
spring. That's a chore I don't much care for, so don't bother. We do
extremely well with delphiniums, planted several colours and the birds
kindly brought in the colours we didn't have - colour range of a
mauve/purple shade.

Roses are tricky - I dug up a wild Alberta Rose, our provincial emblem,
(colour reminiscent of the Queen Alexandra Rose used on the flag day in
the UK). You can grow domestic varities but need a lot of nursing and
protection. We have zcouple in the back, (what's the terminology),
shoots growing up from the ground and one in the front, which our friend
had no success with;  it just likes its mini environment and shoots up
to some 8 feet blocking our friend window - still showing some budes but
we'll have to severly chop it back soon.

Rhododendrohns, azaleas wouldn't stand a chance in hell. Certain days
can be a bit harsh but the winters have considerably diminished since I
came here in '75. And regardless of what Judson or his fellow
anti-greenies have to say, the bloody Arctic is melting and the Artic
countries, (Denmark (Greenland), Canada, Russia,USA, is it Norway ?),
with an interest in the North West Passage concept are vying for water
territorial claims. Surprise, surprise - they disagree on who realy owns
what - Canda/USA as just one example.

Just as a close off, the winters kill off everything dead, dead,
dead..... The grass goes hay-coloured primarily from being oovered with
snow. Then comes the spring, April sometime those flowerbeds look asleep
until suddenly green starts popping up and one of the first - Lily of
the Valley. Slowly the grass takes on a yellowish-green look and
eventually switches to green - but never to the extent of the
multi-coloured greens of UK. The other thing that affects Calgary's
weather is the Chinooks - not the helicopter, the winds. Breezes blow in
from BC wind their way across the mountains, slip into southern Alberta
and a garden can change from snow to semi-green grass in less than 24
hours. Back in April '75 I phoned Eileen, still in UK . "What do you
mean?", she said. "You had snow at breakfast time and now you are out in
the aternoon sunning yourselves !" Perfectly truye - and weather, not
politics, (#2) is the main topic in Calgary :-)

> </non-COBOL related text>
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> I don't understand 'OT'

try Off Topic :-)

I'll get back on the Re-install jazz.  No more below.

Jimmy

> 
> Never mind. While this is drastic (it would be unthinkable for me; my 
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT : Kiwiland

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2008-09-23T17:10:58-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6IeCk.43757$PK.14865@newsfe04.iad>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<6jlmhrF3uguhU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<7300a46c-2e0a-4f13-86d0-bb4d9f7448a2@i24g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <WAHAk.13407$wr1.9652@newsfe02.iad> <6jj5ltF3jh3vU1@mid.individual.net> <hGeBk.15988$rV4.14867@newsfe03.iad> <6jlmhrF3uguhU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:

> <non-COBOL related text.>
> Good for you, Jimmy. I have a number of friends here who've had hip or knee 
> replacements and it gives them a whole new lease of life.

> Be careful with 
> the squatting, if you're gardening, (but I'm sure they've told you what and 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> wine... I need to figure out something I can offer her... :-)

What an idyllic setting you describe, but you didn't tell us you receive
a fee for writing up Kiwiland for the NZ Tourist Board :-).

So I put the goggle box on today and while channel surfing come across a
Travel Chanel; the program is about New Zealand; only watched about 5
minutes as I had other things to do. I assume it was specifically
referring to South Island and some parts which are craggy, snowless,
treeless mountain ranges. So a former Lancastrian (from his accent),
plans to travel from one end of the island to the other. En route he
encounters a Snow Boomer created by the wind flows circling around your
delightful islands. (Forgotten name already - is 'Boomer' correct ?)
Plodding through the snow he follows tracks of two others where he finds
a father and son already in early stages of hypothermia. Interesting
medic fact, he can't wait to put up a tent, which they had with them,
because part of the treatment is NOT to move victims; moving their limbs
can exacerbate the condition. Unable to eat easily he feeds them chocolate.

Then he contacted a rescue service. Appears you have about 40 deaths a
year and on average some 500 searches. I switched off at that point......

Holey Moley we have roughly some 40 deaths a year between BC and Alberta
in the mountain range from south of us all the way up to Jasper. But
ours breaks down into mountain climbing deaths - not necessarily
associated with winter conditions and the majority are avalanche deaths;
some good people get caught, but the majority from skiing in off-limit
sign-posted areas, where they trigger the avalanche. Rescue parties,
plenty of those throughout the year, but not just mountain related and
can simply be people disoriented and lost in the bush. Numbers, I don't
know, but I'd guess 500 wouldn't be unreasonable.

I assume South Island because that's where you have your alpine scenery
and skiing, and of course large areas where the tree line extends
towards mountain tops - as confirmed by the LOTR movie. I loved the
scenery and the camaraderie shown by the team of lead actors but once
the trees started walking, that was a turn-off for me !

Nevertheless Peter Jackson did a great job, as he did with his version
of 'King Kong'. At the time I viewed it I wasn't aware there were two
additional versions 1976 and 2005 since the 1933 original, nor that 
Peter Jackson had directed what I watching, until it finished. What I 
particularly liked was the way he established the sympathetic 
'relationship' between the heroine and Kong.

I can't wait to see his remake of the 'Damn Busters'. Wg. Cdr. Guy
Gibson, (played by Richard Todd), was only 24 at the time - be 
interesting to see how he is portrayed. Plus, Jackson had access to 
SECRET documentation which was not available at the time when the first 
movie was produced, (1955).

Back to gardening - want some zucchinis ? They grow to the size of an
English marrow if not picked early enough. We have a saying here - "If 
you see somebody in a supermarket in August/September, buying zucchinis,
then they obviously haven't got very friendly gardening neighbours".

Jimmy
```

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:6IeCk.43757$PK.14865@newsfe04.iad...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I wish I did. The fact is simply that I love this place. I didn't actually 
realize it until a year or so ago; few of us have time to sit and think 
about where we live, we're too busy taking it for granted.

Having said that, no place I've been (and I've been to a few...) is 
Paradise, including this one.

> So I put the goggle box on today and while channel surfing come across a
> Travel Chanel; the program is about New Zealand; only watched about 5
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> delightful islands. (Forgotten name already - is 'Boomer' correct ?)

I have no idea. TV documentaries made by and for foreigners make it up as 
they go along. And the South Island is a separate planet. I haven't been 
there since I was a teenager. :-)

> Plodding through the snow he follows tracks of two others where he finds
> a father and son already in early stages of hypothermia. Interesting
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> chocolate.

We get idiots (usually visitors... we are brought up to have respect for 
both the beauty and the dangers of our environment) who think it is fine to 
go mountaineering in T-shirts and flip-flops. A certain number die every 
year. It is Nature's way of culling the gene pool.
>
> Then he contacted a rescue service. Appears you have about 40 deaths a
> year and on average some 500 searches. I switched off at that point......

I don't know, but I'd believe it. Mind you, we also have 2,400,000 visitors 
a year who DON'T die on a mountain, and have th etime of their lives, but 
that's not newsworthy
>
> Holey Moley we have roughly some 40 deaths a year between BC and Alberta
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> sign-posted areas, where they trigger the avalanche.

Yep, the same strata of the gene pool I mentioned above. They are 
ubiquitous.

>Rescue parties,
> plenty of those throughout the year, but not just mountain related and
> can simply be people disoriented and lost in the bush. Numbers, I don't
> know, but I'd guess 500 wouldn't be unreasonable.

And you have dangerous animals in the woods. We don't even have snakes here.
>
> I assume South Island because that's where you have your alpine scenery
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the trees started walking, that was a turn-off for me !

Treebeard was certainly not everyone's cup of tea, but I hope you stuck with 
it; there is some brilliant stuff after that.
>
> Nevertheless Peter Jackson did a great job, as he did with his version
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'relationship' between the heroine and Kong.

He had wanted to make that film all his life. It was like an obsession with 
him so when he got the chance he took it with both hands. The academy Awards 
it picked up were well deserved. Jackson's attention to detail is 
unbelievable. (Just as well for the world he never took up computer 
programming; he would have been outstanding at it... :-)) I was watching it 
again the other day and it still hasn't waned.

>
> I can't wait to see his remake of the 'Damn Busters'. Wg. Cdr. Guy
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> produced, (1955).

Yes, that should be good. I understand it is currently in production here, 
'though it is keeping a very low profile.  Jackson is not directing it, he 
is producing it. He is also planning to make "The Hobbit" which is my 
favourite of the Middle Earth stories. It tells the story of Bilbo Baggins 
and how he comes to acquire the Ring of Power  completely by accident, never 
knowing what it is. There will be two movies apparently, one in 2010 and one 
in 2011.
>
> Back to gardening - want some zucchinis ? They grow to the size of an
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Often referred to as "courgettes" here. They pickle quite well.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Kiwiland

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2008-09-23T21:58:04-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gViCk.44095$PK.37523@newsfe04.iad>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<6jtfphF4sontU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<7300a46c-2e0a-4f13-86d0-bb4d9f7448a2@i24g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <WAHAk.13407$wr1.9652@newsfe02.iad> <6jj5ltF3jh3vU1@mid.individual.net> <hGeBk.15988$rV4.14867@newsfe03.iad> <6jlmhrF3uguhU1@mid.individual.net> <6IeCk.43757$PK.14865@newsfe04.iad> <6jtfphF4sontU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
>>What an idyllic setting you describe, but you didn't tell us you receive
>>a fee for writing up Kiwiland for the NZ Tourist Board :-).
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Paradise, including this one.

I understand exactly what you are saying. Born in London and 
subsequently living in Portsmouth from 1939 until I joined the RAF in 
1949, I was trapped in a city environment.

Came out of the RAF in '61 and we made a bee-line for Guildford where 
Eileen's sister lived in nearby Godalming. Southern Surrey, Sussex and 
Hampshire - just plain beautiful. Then it just got better. With Unigate 
we moved to the South West - Yeovil,Somerset, then to Trowbridge, 
Wiltshire from '65 - '67 and luckily back to my beloved Somerset with 
Debenhams in Taunton. (Ironic, my son born in Bristol, Somerset lives in 
a district south west of me called Somerset - but Somerset it is not !).

It's daft, but I got the feeling I was in Arthurian England while living 
there. When I went back, just that once in 2000 I couldn't believe how 
many shades of green there were.

I was fascinated by 'Lorna Doone' which we read as part of English in 
school and via Exmoor we were so close to the legendary Doone Valley. 
"Arrh wun can take herself and see Oare Church where Lorna and Jan Ridd 
gotten themsel's married ".

Alternatively to Lorna Doone Farm (tourist embellishment) where you can 
get scones, raspberry jam and clotted cream. We will always remember 
that from our honeymoon 50 years July 2007 - we arrived on the Lambretta 
scooter, parked it and paid our dues. Up comes this pleasant but scruffy 
lady in a fawn mac (raincoat). We still remember the phrase. Having 
taken a look she said, "Ahh you'm alright. You'm paid".

Short distance east of Taunton, Camel(ot) and a little bit north, before 
you could catch your breath, you car had whisked you through the tiny 
village of Athelney where King Alfred 'burned the cakes' in the 
surrounding marshes. On either side of the road wicker-ware for sale, 
baskets, little tables etc., made from the rushes in the marshes.

Then of course there is Glastonbury Tor and town, where legend has it 
Jospeh of Arimathea brought the Holy Grail and Arthur and his queen were 
supposedly buried. Most of Somerset was marshy until medieval times - 
the monks introducing canal-ing to drain the marshes.

I would have loved to have spent the remainder of my days in the south 
west. Wasn't to be, I had to move on. Calgary, just another big glass 
and concrete city downtown but some of the outlying suburbs are very 
attractively laid out. My community, only three entrances, is called 
Parkland after the Fish Creek Park. Tough sell for developers 
apparently; nobody wanted to live in the south east - just that further 
bit from the mountains. So they made a point of creating a tree-lined 
community. We are just off Parkland Boulevard, which is two-way with a 
centre grass verge and there are three rows of poplar trees left, right 
and centre.

I can sit in my garden surrounded by trees in my own little Heaven on 
Earth which I had to create from scratch. It was just a pile of sandy 
soil when we arrived in '75.

Within forty-five minutes we can be in the mountains west of us. We 
don't do it very often, but ridiculously we go to a village called Bragg 
Creek, where we have a good coffee and bagel which has lox (salmon), 
cream cheese and capers, all in delicious bagels which the owners have 
shipped in from Ontario.

But the scenery, both en route and when surrounded by mountains - I 
mentally take a deep breath each time and gaze at the snow capped 
mountains in wonderment. (This is the Canada I love - with the English 
postcard images of a mountie, an Indian Chief and the spectacular 
scenery). Just as important - many Canadians grumble because Canada is a 
non-event, nothing seems to happen. That's exactly how I like it; no 
hassles. (As for non-events, how many reading this know that we will be 
going into a federal election in October).
> 

>>Back to gardening - want some zucchinis ? They grow to the size of an
>>English marrow if not picked early enough. We have a saying here - "If you 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Often referred to as "courgettes" here. They pickle quite well.

That's what I knew them as in England when I was first introduced in 
late sixties/early seventies. Zucchini I think is from the Italian 
influence in the States. 'Courgette' = 'little squash'; makes sense.
Back in UK I knew the aubergine which is the N.American egg plant. When 
I inadvertently say "aubergine" I get a "What ?". Then I ask, what 
colour is an egg plant ?". Not too many folks understand the question.

The only vegetable I definitely know I can't eat is the aubergine. Not 
tummy pains, but I get Montezuma's Revenge. I have an excellent US 
cook-book, author lives in Idaho, and from that I picked up that sliced 
zucchini can be used as a substitute for egg-plant in Greek type dishes 
- it works.

The cook-book has one of my favourite recipes - Zucchini Pizza. I can 
e-mail you the recipe if interested. But basically, the crust is made 
from grated zucchini, (this is where those large monsters come in handy, 
first having halved them and removed the seeds); drain the zucchini 
otherwise you will get an overflow; mixed with 4 eggs and cheese - 
spread on a pizza pan and bake at 325F for about 20 minutes, until you 
have a semi-golden 'crust' - more or less we are talking about a 
frittata or omelette.  Use your imagination on the topping, canned 
tomatoes cooked with onions, oregano - haven't tried it but you can add 
slices of one of the Italian spiced sausages. The cooked topping is 
spread on the 'crust', then topped with grated cheese and finally 
Parmesan. Bake for another 30 minutes approx.

Jimmy
```

On Wed, 24 Sep 2008 22:44:24 +1200, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

>BTW have you had a look at the new web site? It's taken 140 hits but no one 
>has commented. I don't understand why anyone would visit and not comment, 
>but maybe they think it is terrible and are trying to spare my feelings... 
>:-)

Top Ten Topics on C.L.C.

10. If you ignore it, maybe it will go away.

9. So it's not leaving. I don't care, I'll retire soon.

8. Billions and billions of lines of production code.

7. That's nothing new. 

6. Trivia. 

5. Mainframes.

4. Today's Youth.

3. The evils of contracting companies and/or management.

2. Defending deeply held religious beliefs such as period at the end of a sentence.

1. The Good Old Days.

Well don't know about Robert's list, but I did take a look - curious 
like the cat. Didn't comment, because I wanted to see if anybody else 
ran into the same problem I did.

1. Cosmetic - nice looking web page but do you really expect Bill Klein 
to be able to read your menu bar with a magnifying glass. I could hardly 
read it and I only use glasses for viewing TV, computer screen and 
reading. Give some thought to changing the colour density.

2. Hiccup - not major, but it should point you there; make a selection 
like 'View Source' - I get a blank blue screen (not the MS one but your 
own pale blue) and can't view anything. Might be a problem with my 
machine - but I don't think so - I took a stab at SP3 last night and 
appears it worked; but that doesn't mean my machine is kosher, some more 
checking first.

Now Roberto as you sidled in on the trivia. There was a time when a gent 
mentioned picnicking by a river, bottle of wine, quiche (I think) and a 
lady friend. Recent message, same gent mentioned his wife - that 
girlfriend and wife, same person ?

Jimmy

the frame source and there are no HTML tags or even a DOCTYPE.

This is probably it:

 <iframe src="cobdata.txt" frameborder="1" id="if1" height="750px"
width="742px" scrolling="auto" runat="Server"></iframe>

and when we look at cobdata.txt it is just the source, not even a
<code> .. </code>

"Richard" <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:b0597413-f80c-429f-bef0-a50b1f8f9e36@o40g2000prn.googlegroups.com...
On Sep 25, 11:01 am, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
> Check that you have a copy of Notepad enabled and running on your machine.
>
> What Browser are you using? There may be a problem with Firefox.

I have 'Web Developer' plug-in. When I turn off all CSS styles the
page displays correctly, or at least what would be expected with all
CSS off. The frame is there with the source code and the scroll bar.

Turn the CSS back on and the source dissappears behind a blue panel
that fills the frame, scrollbar and all.

[Pete]

Thanks for that.

Yes, it could well be the CSS theme that is causing a problem. That would 
also account for the blue you are seeing.

These pages are created on the server and they merge a pre-compiled Master 
Page which is controlled by CSS to give a common theme. The result of the 
merge is then served up.

I'll have another look at the "ShowMe" page and check the Master Page it is 
using.

Thanks for your observation.

Pete.
```

On Wed, 24 Sep 2008 12:47:26 -0600, "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:

>2. Hiccup - not major, but it should point you there; make a selection 
>like 'View Source' - I get a blank blue screen (not the MS one but your 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>checking first.

It's a Firefox problem, defaulting frame height to zero. As a workaround, right click on
the first line (you might be able to see half of it), select View Frame Source or This
Frame followed by View Only this frame. It works fine with  IE. Your machine is not at
fault.

>Now Roberto as you sidled in on the trivia. There was a time when a gent 
>mentioned picnicking by a river, bottle of wine, quiche (I think) and a 
>lady friend. Recent message, same gent mentioned his wife - that 
>girlfriend and wife, same person ?

I had to look it up. That was in 4/2002, when I lived in Northport NY. I didn't mention a
river; picnic would have been on the Long Island Sound. Yes, same person.

We've lived in 15 US cities since 1998.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Kiwiland

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-09-24T22:38:57-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<k71md4trfgoctsrv494k01989v0a8javsh@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<WAHAk.13407$wr1.9652@newsfe02.iad> <6jj5ltF3jh3vU1@mid.individual.net> <hGeBk.15988$rV4.14867@newsfe03.iad> <6jlmhrF3uguhU1@mid.individual.net> <6IeCk.43757$PK.14865@newsfe04.iad> <6jtfphF4sontU1@mid.individual.net> <gViCk.44095$PK.37523@newsfe04.iad> <6juk09F54u50U1@mid.individual.net> <v4ekd49gh3c2npvov83kd8tmnc69vjrhsm@4ax.com> <3XvCk.30870$wr1.23408@newsfe02.iad> <k2tld4lknn9n8s5bf02ta8obp5trq86guh@4ax.com> <TfDCk.31005$wr1.13348@newsfe02.iad>`

```
On Wed, 24 Sep 2008 21:07:31 -0600, "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:

>Thanks for reply Robert and the tips on Firefox - but I have to say WHY 
>? WHY ?, WHY ? - there surely have to be an integrated set of standards 
>for Browsers, and if it is specifically a Firefox programming bug, which 
>you personally have found, why no update from Mozilla ?

Default frame height is not specified in the HTML 4.01 Specification. Firefox defaults to
zero; IE defaults to 100%. The fix is to specify 100%.

We were aware of the problem.

>Well congrats - and a sincere happy life to you and your wife. Having 
>written the message I subsequently thought, 'Oh my gawd he could 
>interpret it incorrectly ' :-
 
Once, I was buying cheap costume jewelry in a truck stop. The clerk made idle conversation
by asking "Is this for your girlfriend?" I answered, "My girlfriend doesn't wear such
junk; it's for my wife."  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Kiwiland

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-09-25T17:37:39+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6k0md3F5aotdU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<WAHAk.13407$wr1.9652@newsfe02.iad> <6jj5ltF3jh3vU1@mid.individual.net> <hGeBk.15988$rV4.14867@newsfe03.iad> <6jlmhrF3uguhU1@mid.individual.net> <6IeCk.43757$PK.14865@newsfe04.iad> <6jtfphF4sontU1@mid.individual.net> <gViCk.44095$PK.37523@newsfe04.iad> <6juk09F54u50U1@mid.individual.net> <v4ekd49gh3c2npvov83kd8tmnc69vjrhsm@4ax.com> <3XvCk.30870$wr1.23408@newsfe02.iad> <k2tld4lknn9n8s5bf02ta8obp5trq86guh@4ax.com> <TfDCk.31005$wr1.13348@newsfe02.iad> <k71md4trfgoctsrv494k01989v0a8javsh@4ax.com>`

```


"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:k71md4trfgoctsrv494k01989v0a8javsh@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 24 Sep 2008 21:07:31 -0600, "James J. Gavan" 
> <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> We were aware of the problem.

Robert did tell me about this before we published the site. I specifically 
set the height and width attributes of the iframe to be compatible with the 
CSS frame it is appearing in.

Here's a snippet of the pertinent C# code from the code-behind page:

        // set the Iframe dynamically...
        frame1.Attributes["src"]= pcontent;
        frame1.Attributes["frameborder"] = "1";
        frame1.Attributes["id"] = "if1";
        frame1.Attributes["height"] = "750px";
        frame1.Attributes["width"] = "742px";
        frame1.Attributes["scrolling"] = "auto";
        frame1.Attributes["runat"] = "Server";

            theFrameHolder.Controls.Add(frame1);

This is part of a pre-compiled routine that finds out what we are 
displaying, and sets up an iframe to display it. Whether you do it in 
JavaScript on the client, or C# on the server( as in this case), it is 
pretty standard web programming. I just tested it with VS2008's cross 
Browser compatibility tool and it found no problem.

I have no idea why this doesn't work on Firefox. If anyone can shed some 
light on it, I'd appreciate it.

Cheers,

Pete.
```

```
On Sep 24, 10:44 pm, "Pete Dashwood"

> It's taken 140 hits but no one
> has commented. I don't understand why anyone would visit and not comment,
> but maybe they think it is terrible and are trying to spare my feelings...
> :-)

That volume may be what is expected from zombie 'pwned' Windows robots
looking for IIS servers to infect.

I get that sort of level and there is nothing on my site nor any links
to it, it is purely internal.

Interestingly it has a high level of Win98 with IE 5.5 from around the
world, only the occational XP, and a couple of Vista. It isn't people
doing this.
```

I visited today from a compter with IE 7 but that uses a higher screen 
resolution than what I use on my home computer.  The "C. Dashwood" part of 
the copyright line was cut off  and the logon boxes extend outside the 
frame.  This does not happen at home.

Even after registering I could not download either executables or source 
code. I just tried again from home with the same result. Perhaps I am too 
dense or I am missing something.  None of  "Home", "Download executables", 
or "Download  source" worked from any page.

For the COBOL code I could SELECT ALL and then COPY to the clipboard and the 
PASTE to Notepad.  For the other three (2 sceenshots and 1 process diagram) 
I did a Save Picture As and they printed quite nicely on the color laser 
printer at work.

I would like to download the execuatble and try it out, but I am not sure 
what I am doing wrong.  I just tried agan from Firefox but nothing happens 
when I click on the download link.

Also I just now noticed on the logon from Firefox that I could make a 
comment from the logon page.  I did not see that before.  You might get more 
comments if you allow people to do it from other pages as well.  As one 
commercial on the radio around here says: "just a thought not a sermon" :-)
```

"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote in message 
news:98-dneTluvhwz0DVnZ2dnUVZ_rbinZ2d@earthlink.com...
>
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well, I considered having a ruler jump out of the keyboard and rap you over 
the knuckles, but that technology is not quite there...yet. :-)

I gave a lot of thought to the logon pages and they are not just "standard 
JavaScript" but actually custom tailored to do what I wanted. I hate logging 
on to sites and it is really annoying when you have entered everything and 
then it turns out you have to start from scratch because you're not 
registered. (Who can remember which sites they HAVE registered with...I 
don't even try...) I therefore made an effort to let people have whatever 
password structure they want (why should I judge their security? There are 
no lives at stake here...), and NOT have to rekey their userid.

The downside is that if you get it wrong, you will need to go back to square 
one. The upside is that most people get it right... :-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Kiwiland

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-09-27T00:45:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gbjvnb$efb$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<7300a46c-2e0a-4f13-86d0-bb4d9f7448a2@i24g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <6k37mmF5rsfkU1@mid.individual.net> <98-dneTluvhwz0DVnZ2dnUVZ_rbinZ2d@earthlink.com> <6k5c17F66seoU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <6k5c17F66seoU1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>I gave a lot of thought to the logon pages and they are not just "standard 
>JavaScript" but actually custom tailored to do what I wanted.

[snip]

>The downside is that if you get it wrong, you will need to go back to square 
>one.

... but hey, if that's the custom tailoring you want, well and good.  Me, 
I was taught differently; from 
<http://groups.google.com/group/comp.lang.cobol/msg/a3591bab00cdeef3?dmode=source>

--begin quoted text:

As my first programming instructor asked the class:

'What is the definition of a 'computer user'?

(pause for responses, each met with a curt 'No')

'A 'computer user' is someone who, when prompted to supply a 'Y' or 'N' 
response, will key in the numeral '8'.  Write *all* of your online 
applications with this in mind and your users will love you.'

Life may not be that simple, no... but I've found it a good rule to 
remember.

--end quoted text

DD
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6k6v05F6d5caU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<7300a46c-2e0a-4f13-86d0-bb4d9f7448a2@i24g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <WAHAk.13407$wr1.9652@newsfe02.iad> <6jj5ltF3jh3vU1@mid.individual.net> <hGeBk.15988$rV4.14867@newsfe03.iad> <6jlmhrF3uguhU1@mid.individual.net> <6IeCk.43757$PK.14865@newsfe04.iad> <6jtfphF4sontU1@mid.individual.net> <gViCk.44095$PK.37523@newsfe04.iad> <6juk09F54u50U1@mid.individual.net> <9433ef61-bae1-4532-8028-5565f63ce5f7@q26g2000prq.googlegroups.com> <6jvvc5F5f3arU1@mid.individual.net> <NIqdnVT81d0Dh0HVnZ2dnUVZ_gOdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <8aOdnSUklvkmgUHVnZ2dnUVZ_gidnZ2d@earthlink.com> <f6KdnZBjFLe8u0HVnZ2dnUVZ_uKdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <PpmdnU5o9seU00HVnZ2dnUVZ_s3inZ2d@earthlink.com> <6k37mmF5rsfkU1@mid.individual.net> <98-dneTluvhwz0DVnZ2dnUVZ_rbinZ2d@earthlink.com> <6k5c17F66seoU1@mid.individual.net> <UrGdnUgCxuJgL0DVnZ2dnUVZ_qHinZ2d@earthlink.com> <6k61cmF68985U1@mid.individual.net> <7bWdnbJ2PNq83EPVnZ2dnUVZ_jqdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```


"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote in message 
news:7bWdnbJ2PNq83EPVnZ2dnUVZ_jqdnZ2d@earthlink.com...
>
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
…[59 more quoted lines elided]…
> I am just reporting my experiences, which I thought you asked for.

Yes, I did, and I appreciate you, and all the other people doing it. It is 
impossible for me to improve something if iI don't know it is broken, or 
even less than easy for people to use.

My comment was tongue-in-cheek, Charlie.

>It is your web site so I think you should have it the way you want.  Either 
>way I will probably access it to pick up any changes made to the software. 
>It is not a big deal to me.  I am not telling you to do anything or when to 
>do it. You set your own priorities.  After all it is a free service so user 
>complaints are not really warranted.

It is pretty much as I wanted it. I designed it then built it to that 
design. But without the rich experiences of other people it would be a 
sterile and fairly useless site. I don't mind people complaining (although 
I'd prefer the complaints were fair - for the most part, they have been...) 
as it gives me a chance to see things that others notice. I had a girl who 
builds web sites take a look around it and she broke it in 5 minutes. I had 
forgotten to do some stuff and figured no-one would spot it anyway...she 
went straight to it like a bloody exocet and succeeded in getting into 
another site that should have been secure and inaccessible from this site... 
(it is now...) :-). It was a salutary lesson to me. No, it is nice when 
people say nice things and enjoy the experience, but it is really useful 
when they don't. This site is better than many I've built. The next one will 
be better than this one. It's a bit like programming, really... :-)

Pete.
```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:6k6v05F6d5caU1@mid.individual.net...
>
>
…[96 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Programmers tend to be a critical bunch and are often hardest on themselves. 
Too critical.  Too perfectionist.  It helps with the work but can be hard on 
people.

I am tyring to get more fit and trim.  I had lost more than 50 lbs before we 
lost our baby but with all the stress I gained it all back. In the process I 
am getting some psychological help.  When I described my successes and 
"failures" (actually opportunities to learn) the feedback I got  is that I 
am usually too hard on myself and I should congratulate myself more on my 
successes.  I suggest that you do the same.  I don't see very many people 
(including myself, even though I have had lots of ideas along that line) 
making useful and free resources available. Nothing is ever perfect but you 
can be proud of what you have done.
```

- **References:** `<7300a46c-2e0a-4f13-86d0-bb4d9f7448a2@i24g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <WAHAk.13407$wr1.9652@newsfe02.iad> <6jj5ltF3jh3vU1@mid.individual.net> <hGeBk.15988$rV4.14867@newsfe03.iad> <6jlmhrF3uguhU1@mid.individual.net> <6IeCk.43757$PK.14865@newsfe04.iad> <6jtfphF4sontU1@mid.individual.net> <gViCk.44095$PK.37523@newsfe04.iad> <6juk09F54u50U1@mid.individual.net> <9433ef61-bae1-4532-8028-5565f63ce5f7@q26g2000prq.googlegroups.com> <6jvvc5F5f3arU1@mid.individual.net> <NIqdnVT81d0Dh0HVnZ2dnUVZ_gOdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <8aOdnSUklvkmgUHVnZ2dnUVZ_gidnZ2d@earthlink.com> <f6KdnZBjFLe8u0HVnZ2dnUVZ_uKdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote in message 
news:f6KdnZBjFLe8u0HVnZ2dnUVZ_uKdnZ2d@earthlink.com...
>
> "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote in message 
…[56 more quoted lines elided]…
> but it does not say why.

If the application runs OK, this is an error in my deployment XML.

I check a particular registry value to see if the component has registered. 
Unfortunately, it seems to register in different registry keys on different 
machines. I need to review this, but simply don't have time at the moment.

The "fix" (if it is required; if the application runs then it isn't...) is 
to do the following:

1. open a DOS box
2.  type the following command:

regsvr32 c:\windows\system32\cobstruct.dll

(Note: the path above may vary slightly on different versions of windows. 
The path shown is for XP. If you're not sure, do a find on the .dll and note 
where it is located...)

You should receive a popup saying the component has registered successfully.

You are good to go.

Pete.
```

<a id="t-b06dc09bc2"></a>
## Thread: Is your next language COBOL? — 7 msgs

### Is your next language COBOL?

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-09-20T15:27:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u-qdnZrbzOgvwUjVnZ2dnUVZ_hydnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
Well, not YOURS, but the hippie down the hall.

Somebody's got to maintain a quarter of a trillion lines of code of the most 
common programming language in the 21st century.

http://www.ddj.com/architect/210602491?pgno=1
```

#### ↳ Re: Is your next language COBOL?

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-09-21T11:21:00+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6jleqsF3o3bvU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<u-qdnZrbzOgvwUjVnZ2dnUVZ_hydnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```


"HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com> wrote in message 
news:u-qdnZrbzOgvwUjVnZ2dnUVZ_hydnZ2d@earthlink.com...
> Well, not YOURS, but the hippie down the hall.
>
> Somebody's got to maintain a quarter of a trillion lines of code of the 
> most common programming language in the 21st century.

Or they would (perhaps...), if that were true...

It is, at best, disputable (the Gartner "statistics" on which it is based 
have no sound statistical basis, even before you consider that Gartner are 
an "interested party"), at worst, it is just plain wrong.

But really, it doesn't matter.

The code can be, and is being in many places, refactored into objects. The 
automation of this process is something I'll be talking more about in the 
coming week with an example of legacy COBOL running in .NET, without the 
need for a .NET compiler.

>
>
> http://www.ddj.com/architect/210602491?pgno=1
>

"How are the mighty fallen..."

Doctor Dobbs used to be reliable and accurate.  According to this article, 
which was, on the whole, balanced, IBM have implemented the COBOL 2002 
standard, OO COBOL was invented by the COBOL 2002 standard, [which habitual 
readers here will remember was originally COBOL 98], (the people who sweated 
it in 1998 will be pissed off; no recognition whatsoever...), and a number 
of other minor rewrites of history.

The problem I have with this is not about COBOL. It is the fact that kids 
reading it will think it is true.

Pete.
```

What the hell. Harvest it - I don't mind giving it a shot :-)

Just ate something from the Philippines yesterday, Dole fruit salad in a
plastic screw-top jar. Delicious !

Jimmy

*>--------------------------------------------------------------------

Note for Pete Dashwood :

Instead of sending this message from Draft I inadvertently sent my Draft 
reply to you about gardening - so it's un-edited and I did mean to add 
the following which I'm sure will give you a laugh.

International Express (UK) did an article on Rex Harrison, the likable 
persona on screen wasn't very likable in real life. He was married 6 
times. Having made 'The Agony and the Ecstasy' as Pope Julius II with 
Charlton Heston playing Michelangelo, the latter, after filming referred 
to Harrison as a "bloody asshole".

Harrison starred on Broadway in 'My Fair Lady', (as Professor Higgins), 
with Julie Andrews (Eliza) and Stanley Holloway (as the dustman). Rex 
was disliked by stars, chorus and stage-hands, but when his contract 
finished threw a farewell party. Holloway reported that the whole team 
was a no-show at Harrison's shindig and he threatened to sue Holloway.

A few days before closing Harrison was leaving the theatre with 
Holloway. An elderly woman admirer came up excitedly brandishing her 
theatre programme, and "Would Mr. Harrison kindly autograph my programme ?"

According to Stanley Holloway, Harrison told her to "Push off!' - maybe 
the original was a more expressive expletive. Indignant, the lady 
started to slap Harrison with the programme.

As Stanley Holloway observed, "That was the first time the fan hit the 
shit".
```

```
On Sat, 20 Sep 2008 15:27:25 -0500, "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com>
wrote:

>Somebody's got to maintain a quarter of a trillion lines of code of the most 
>common programming language in the 21st century.

Quit a bit of that won't be maintained.   

There used to be TV and radio repair shops in most towns.    Radios
needed to be maintained.    Now people throw away printers because it
costs as much to have someone look at them (before repair), as it does
to replace them.

Code has been moving that way for some time which process is speeding
up.

That doesn't mean there won't be maintenance - but it will be at a
different level, where coding languages matter less.
```

---

<a id="t-c929c5c8ab"></a>
## Thread: Freeware tool and code to ponder on... — 81 this month / 107 total

### Freeware tool and code to ponder on...

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-09-23T14:44:12+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6jr3fmF4lc1iU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
A few weeks ago, I asked if anyone had a tool for calculating offsets and 
lengths "on-the-fly" in COBOL record layouts.

There were a number of responses and they were generally helpful, but Robert 
Wagner had a very nice little engine which he kindly agreed to make 
Freeware.

Robert posted his code and you can find it under the "cobdata" thread of 
this forum.

I had offered to wrap any suitable code and put it on .NET so we have been 
working together to get a deliverable product since then.

This exercise has been very interesting. I have never met Robert, just as I 
have never met most of you, but we had no problem working together to sort 
out the problems in bringing our little project to fruition.

There is such a wealth of talent here it would be good to see more joint 
working and sharing of different skill sets.

For myself, I will happily participate in and contribute to any such 
projects, on the understanding that it cannot be a full time effort and time 
may not always be available. (Think "back burner" projects, that really 
don't have a critical deadline).

This project has benefits far and beyond the provision of a simple little 
tool.

We are offering total access to all sources, so people can see how various 
effects were achieved. You can cut and paste our code, change it, re-use it, 
and are encouraged to do so.

I'm always rattling on about expanded skill sets. This project required the 
following (and you have full  download access to all of it...):

For the tool ("COBOL data structure analyser"):

1. Standard procedural COBOL.
2. OO COBOL.
3. C#

For the Deployment and the web site:

1. ASP.NET
2. JavaScript
3. Windows Script Hosting (VBScript)
4. C#
5. XML

The only tools I used were Fujitsu COBOL IDE and Visual Studio 2008 (which 
never stops amazing me as I find new stuff in it :-)), Robert may have used 
some other tools.

Conceptually, the project shows how Legacy COBOL can be quite easily 
leveraged onto a modern platform (.NET) and can play there with Classes and 
Objects written in other languages.

Please visit the Web site (it is intended to support this community) and 
download the tool. Remember when you run it, this is COBOL...:-)

ALL comments and observations should be posted to this forum. :-)

Here's the link: http://primacomputing.co.nz/cobdata

Enjoy!

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Here's one problem

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-09-24T15:47:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<76ednR2xbZzbOkfVnZ2dnUVZ_sTinZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<6jr3fmF4lc1iU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
The following code:
...
  10 NSMAST-AUX PIC X.
  10 NSMAST--SO PIC X. *>redundant 8/06
  10 NSMAST-SYSTEM-SPARE PIC X(6).
05 NSMAST-ABOUT.
  10 NSMAST-IMPRINT PIC X(20).
...

Decodes as follows:

  30 10 nsmast-aux                      315   1
  31 10 nsmast--so                      316   1
  32 0* redundant                       317   6
  33 05 nsmast-about                    323 192
  34 10 nsmast-imprint                  323  20

Note the comment ("*>") is not handled correctly. Also the casing of the 
original is not honored.

I suspect that the funny way it handles comments also causes a miscount of 
the record size. In the case here, your program reports a size of 1655 bytes 
when the actual size is 2232.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Here's one problem

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-09-25T10:48:44+12:00

- **Message-ID:** `<6jvuedF5em0rU1@mid.individual.net>`
